1.1: In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God.
1.2: The same was in the beginning with God.
1.3: All things were made by him; and without him was not any thing made that was made.
1.4: In him was life; and the life was the light of men.
1.5: And the light shineth in darkness; and the darkness comprehended it not.
1.6: There was a man sent from God, whose name was John.
1.7: The same came for a witness, to bear witness of the Light, that all men through him might believe.
1.8: He was not that Light, but was sent to bear witness of that Light.
1.9: That was the true Light, which lighteth every man that cometh into the world.
1.10: He was in the world, and the world was made by him, and the world knew him not.
1.11: He came unto his own, and his own received him not.
1.12: But as many as received him, to them gave he power to become the sons of God, even to them that believe on his name:
1.13: Which were born, not of blood, nor of the will of the flesh, nor of the will of man, but of God.
1.14: And the Word was made flesh, and dwelt among us, and we beheld his glory, the glory as of the only begotten of the Father, full of grace and truth.
1.15: John bare witness of him, and cried, saying, This was he of whom I spake, He that cometh after me is preferred before me: for he was before me.
1.16: And of his fulness have all we received, and grace for grace.
1.17: For the law was given by Moses, but grace and truth came by Jesus Christ.
1.18: No man hath seen God at any time, the only begotten Son, which is in the bosom of the Father, he hath declared him.

2.1: In the fifteenth year of the reign of Tiberius Caesar, Pontius Pilate being governor of Judaea, and Herod being tetrarch of Galilee, and his brother Philip tetrarch of Ituraea and of the region of Trachonitis, and Lysanias the tetrarch of Abilene,
2.2: Annas and Caiaphas being the high priests, the word of God came unto John the son of Zacharias in the wilderness.
2.3: Jesus descended out of heaven into the Galilean city of Capernaum, a city near the sea, in the borders of Zabulon and Nephthalim.
2.4: Having taken on the appearance of a man, he appeared thirty years of age and was teaching in the synagogue on the Sabbath days.
2.5: And he began to say unto them, This day is this scripture fulfilled in your ears.
2.6: And they were astonished at his doctrine: for his word was with power.
2.7: And in the synagogue there was a man, which had a spirit of an unclean devil, and cried out with a loud voice,
2.8: Saying, let us alone; what have we to do with thee, thou Jesus? art thou come to destroy us? I know thee who thou art; the Holy One of God.
2.9: And Jesus rebuked him, saying, Hold thy peace, and come out of him. And when the devil had thrown him in the midst, he came out of him, and hurt him not.
2.10: And they were all amazed, and spake among themselves, saying, What a word is this! for with authority and power he commandeth the unclean spirits, and they come out.
2.11: And the fame of him went out into every place of the country round about.
2.12: And he arose out of the synagogue, and entered into Simon’s house. And Simon’s wife’s mother was taken with a great fever; and they besought him for her.
2.13: And he stood over her, and rebuked the fever; and it left her: and immediately she arose and ministered unto them.
2.14: And the Jews’ passover was at hand, and Jesus went up to Jerusalem.
2.15: And found in the temple those that sold oxen and sheep and doves, and the changers of money sitting:
2.16: And when he had made a scourge of small cords, he drove them all out of the temple, and the sheep, and the oxen; and poured out the changers’ money, and overthrew the tables;
2.17: And said unto them that sold doves, Take these things hence;
2.18: Then answered the Jews and said unto him, What sign shewest thou unto us, seeing that thou doest these things?
2.19: Jesus answered and said unto them, I am come to abolish the sacrifices, if ye cease not from sacrificing, the wrath will not cease from you.
2.20: Destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up.
2.21: Then said the Jews, Forty and six years was this temple in building, and wilt thou rear it up in three days?
2.22: But he spake of the temple of his body.
2.23: Then spake Jesus again unto them, saying, I am the light of the world: he that followeth me shall not walk in darkness, but shall have the light of life.
2.24: The Pharisees therefore said unto him, Thou bearest record of thyself; thy record is not true.
2.25: Jesus answered and said unto them, Though I bear record of myself, yet my record is true: for I know whence I came, and whither I go; but ye cannot tell whence I come, and whither I go.
2.26: Ye judge after the flesh; I judge no man.
2.27: And yet if I judge, my judgment is true: for I am not alone, but I and the Father that sent me.
2.28: It is also written in your law, that the testimony of two men is true.
2.29: I am one that bear witness of myself, and the Father that sent me beareth witness of me.
2.30: Then said they unto him, Where is thy Father? Jesus answered, Ye neither know me, nor my Father: if ye had known me, ye should have known my Father also.
2.31: These words spake Jesus in the treasury, as he taught in the temple: and no man laid hands on him; for his hour was not yet come.
2.32: Then said Jesus again unto them, I go my way, and ye shall seek me, and shall die in your sins: whither I go, ye cannot come.
2.33: Then said the Jews, Will he kill himself? because he saith, Whither I go, ye cannot come.
2.34: And he said unto them, Ye are from beneath; I am from above: ye are of this world; I am not of this world.
2.35: I said therefore unto you, that ye shall die in your sins: for if ye believe not that I am he, ye shall die in your sins.
2.36: Then said they unto him, Who art thou? And Jesus saith unto them, Even the same that I said unto you from the beginning.
2.37: I have many things to say and to judge of you: but he that sent me is true; and I speak to the world those things which I have heard of him.
2.38: They understood not that he spake to them of the Father.
2.39: Then said Jesus unto them, When ye have lifted up the Son of man, then shall ye know that I am he, and that I do nothing of myself; but as my Father hath taught me, I speak these things.
2.40: And he that sent me is with me: the Father hath not left me alone; for I do always those things that please him.
2.41: As he spake these words, many believed on him.
2.42: Now when he was in Jerusalem at the passover, in the feast day, many believed in his name, when they saw the miracles which he did.
2.43: But Jesus did not commit himself unto them, because he knew all men,
2.44: And needed not that any should testify of man: for he knew what was in man.
2.45: There was a man of the Pharisees, named Nicodemus, a ruler of the Jews:
2.46: The same came to Jesus by night, and said unto him, Rabbi, we know that thou art a teacher come from God: for no man can do these miracles that thou doest, except God be with him.
2.47: Jesus answered and said unto him, Verily, verily, I say unto thee, Except a man be born again, he cannot see the kingdom of God.
2.48: Nicodemus saith unto him, How can a man be born when he is old? can he enter the second time into his mother’s womb, and be born?
2.49: Jesus answered, Verily, verily, I say unto thee, Except a man be born of water and of the Spirit, he cannot enter into the kingdom of God.
2.50: That which is born of the flesh is flesh; and that which is born of the Spirit is spirit.
2.51: Marvel not that I said unto thee, Ye must be born again.
2.52: The wind bloweth where it listeth, and thou hearest the sound thereof, but canst not tell whence it cometh, and whither it goeth: so is every one that is born of the Spirit.
2.53: Nicodemus answered and said unto him, How can these things be?
2.54: Jesus answered and said unto him, Art thou a master of Israel, and knowest not these things?
2.55: Verily, verily, I say unto thee, We speak that we do know, and testify that we have seen; and ye receive not our witness.
2.56: If I have told you earthly things, and ye believe not, how shall ye believe, if I tell you of heavenly things?
2.57: And no man hath ascended up to heaven, but he that came down from heaven, even the Son of man which is in heaven.
2.58: And as Moses lifted up the serpent in the wilderness, even so must the Son of man be lifted up:
2.59: That whosoever believeth in him should not perish, but have eternal life.
2.60: For God so loved the world, that he gave his only begotten Son, that whosoever believeth in him should not perish, but have everlasting life.
2.61: For God sent not his Son into the world to condemn the world; but that the world through him might be saved.
2.62: He that believeth on him is not condemned: but he that believeth not is condemned already, because he hath not believed in the name of the only begotten Son of God.
2.63: And this is the condemnation, that light is come into the world, and men loved darkness rather than light, because their deeds were evil.
2.64: For every one that doeth evil hateth the light, neither cometh to the light, lest his deeds should be reproved.
2.65: But he that doeth truth cometh to the light, that his deeds may be made manifest, that they are wrought in God.
2.66: After these things came Jesus and his disciples into the land of Judaea; and there he tarried with them, and baptized.
2.67: And John also was baptizing in Aenon near to Salim, because there was much water there: and they came, and were baptized.
2.68: For John was not yet cast into prison.
2.69: Then there arose a question between some of John’s disciples and the Jews about purifying.
2.70: And they came unto John, and said unto him, Rabbi, he that was with thee beyond Jordan, to whom thou barest witness, behold, the same baptizeth, and all men come to him.
2.71: John answered and said, A man can receive nothing, except it be given him from heaven.
2.72: Ye yourselves bear me witness, that I said, I am not the Christ, but that I am sent before him.
2.73: He that hath the bride is the bridegroom: but the friend of the bridegroom, which standeth and heareth him, rejoiceth greatly because of the bridegroom’s voice: this my joy therefore is fulfilled.
2.74: He must increase, but I must decrease.
2.75: He that cometh from above is above all: he that is of the earth is earthly, and speaketh of the earth: he that cometh from heaven is above all.
2.76: And what he hath seen and heard, that he testifieth; and no man receiveth his testimony.
2.77: He that hath received his testimony hath set to his seal that God is true.
2.78: For he whom God hath sent speaketh the words of God: for God giveth not the Spirit by measure unto him.
2.79: The Father loveth the Son, and hath given all things into his hand.
2.80: He that believeth on the Son hath everlasting life: and he that believeth not the Son shall not see life; but the wrath of God abideth on him.
2.81: But when he saw many of the Pharisees and Sadducees come to his baptism, he said unto them, O generation of vipers, who hath warned you to flee from the wrath to come?
2.82: Bring forth therefore fruits meet for repentance:
2.83: And think not to say within yourselves, We have Abraham to our father: for I say unto you, that God is able of these stones to raise up children unto Abraham.
2.84: And now also the axe is laid unto the root of the trees: therefore every tree which bringeth not forth good fruit is hewn down, and cast into the fire.

3.1: And Jesus returned in the power of the Spirit into Galilee: and there went out a fame of him through all the region round about.
3.2: And he taught in their synagogues, being glorified of all.
3.3: And he came to Nazareth, and, as the custom was,
3.4: He went into the synagogue on the Sabbath day,
3.5: And began to preach to them.
3.6: And he sat down, and the eyes of all who were in the synagogue, were fastened on him.
3.7: And he began to spake to them. And all bare him witness, and wondered at the gracious words which proceeded out of his mouth.
3.8: And he said unto them, Ye will surely say unto me this proverb, Physician, heal thyself: whatsoever we have heard done in Capernaum, do also here in thy country.
3.9: And he said, Verily I say unto you, No prophet is accepted in his own country.
3.10: But I tell you of a truth, many widows were in Israel in the days of Elias, when the heaven was shut up three years and six months, when great famine was throughout all the land;
3.11: But unto none of them was Elias sent, save unto Sarepta, a city of Sidon, unto a woman that was a widow.
3.12: And many lepers were in Israel in the time of Elisha the prophet; and none of them was cleansed, saving Naaman the Syrian.
3.13: And they in the synagogue, when they heard these things, were filled with wrath,
3.14: And rose up, and thrust him out of the city, and led him unto the brow of the hill whereon their city was built, that they might cast him down headlong.
3.15: But he passing through the midst of them went his way to Capernaum.
3.16: Now when the sun was setting, all they that had any sick with divers diseases brought them unto him; and he laid his hands on every one of them, and healed them.
3.17: And devils also came out of many, crying out, and saying, Thou art the Son of God. And he rebuking them suffered them not to speak:
3.18: And when it was day, he departed and went into a desert place: and the people sought him, and came unto him, and stayed him, that he should not depart from them.
3.19: And he said unto them, I must preach the kingdom of God to other cities also: for therefore am I sent.
3.20: And he preached in the synagogues of the Jews.
3.21: And it came to pass, that, as the people pressed upon him to hear the word of God, he stood by the lake of Gennesaret,
3.22: And saw two ships standing by the lake: but the fishermen were gone out of them, and were washing their nets.
3.23: And he entered into one of the ships, which was Simon’s, and prayed him that he would thrust out a little from the land. And he sat down, and taught the people out of the ship.
3.24: Now when he had left speaking, he said unto Simon, Launch out into the deep, and let down your nets for a draught.
3.25: And Simon answering said unto him, Teacher, we have toiled all the night, and have taken nothing: nevertheless at thy word I will let down the net.
3.26: And when they had this done, they inclosed a great multitude of fishes: and their net brake.
3.27: And they beckoned unto their partners, which were in the other ship, that they should come and help them. And they came, and filled both the ships, so that they began to sink.
3.28: When Simon Peter saw it, he fell down at Jesus’ knees, saying, Depart from me; for I am a sinful man, O Lord.
3.29: For he was astonished, and all that were with him, at the draught of the fishes which they had taken:
3.30: And so was also James, and John, the sons of Zebedee, which were partners with Simon. And Jesus said unto Simon, Fear not; from henceforth thou shalt catch men.
3.31: And when they had brought their ships to land, they forsook all, and followed him.
3.32: And it came to pass, when he was in a certain city, behold a man full of leprosy: who seeing Jesus fell on his face, and besought him, saying, Lord, if thou wilt, thou canst make me clean.
3.33: And he put forth his hand, and touched him, saying, I will: be thou clean. And immediately the leprosy departed from him.
3.34: And he charged him to tell no man: but go, and shew thyself to the priest.
3.35: But so much the more went there a fame abroad of him: and great multitudes came together to hear, and to be healed by him of their infirmities.
3.36: And he was withdrawing himself into the wilderness, and praying.
3.37: And it came to pass on a certain day, as he was teaching, that there were Pharisees and doctors of the law sitting by, which were come out of every town of Galilee, and Judaea, and Jerusalem: and the power of the Lord was present to heal them.
3.38: And, behold, men brought in a bed a man which was taken with the palsy: and they sought means to bring him in, and to lay him before him.
3.39: And when they could not find by what way they might bring him in because of the multitude, they went upon the housetop, and let him down through the tiling with his couch into the midst before Jesus.
3.40: And when he saw their faith, he said unto him, Man, thy sins are forgiven thee.
3.41: And the scribes and the Pharisees began to reason, saying, Who is this which speaketh blasphemies? Who can forgive sins, but God alone?
3.42: But when Jesus perceived their thoughts, he answering said unto them, What reason ye in your hearts?
3.43: Whether is easier, to say, Thy sins be forgiven thee; or to say, Rise up and walk?
3.44: But that ye may know that the Son of man hath power upon earth to forgive sins, he said unto the sick of the palsy, I say unto thee, Arise, and take up thy couch, and go into thine house.
3.45: And immediately he rose up before them, and took up that whereon he lay, and departed to his own house, glorifying God.
3.46: And they were all amazed, and they glorified God, and were filled with fear, saying, We have seen strange things to day.
3.47: And after these things he went forth, and saw a publican, named Levi, sitting at the receipt of custom: and he said unto him, Follow me.
3.48: And he left all, rose up, and followed him.
3.49: And Levi made him a great feast in his own house: and there was a great company of publicans and of others that sat down with them.
3.50: But their scribes and Pharisees murmured against his disciples, saying, Why do you eat and drink with publicans and sinners?
3.51: And Jesus answering said unto them, They that are whole need not a physician; but they that are sick.
3.52: I came not to call the righteous, but sinners to repentance.
3.53: And they said unto him, Why do the disciples of John fast often, and make prayers, and likewise the disciples of the Pharisees; but thine eat and drink?
3.54: And he said unto them, Can you make the children of the bride chamber fast, while the bridegroom is with them?
3.55: But the days will come, when the bridegroom shall be taken away from them, and then shall they fast in those days.
3.56: And he spake also a parable unto them;
3.57: No man putteth new wine into old wineskins; else the new wine will burst the wineskins, and be spilled, and the wineskins shall perish.
3.58: But new wine must be put into new wineskins;
3.59: And no man putteth a piece of a new garment upon an old; if otherwise, then both the new maketh a rent, and the piece that was taken out of the new agreeth not with the old.

4.1: And it came to pass on the second sabbath after the first, that he went through the corn fields; and his disciples were hungry, and plucked the ears of corn, rubbing them in their hands.
4.2: And certain of the Pharisees said unto them, Why do ye that which is not lawful to do on the sabbath days?
4.3: And Jesus answering them said, Have ye not read so much as this, what David did, when himself was an hungered, and they which were with him;
4.4: He went into the house of God, and did take and eat the shewbread, and gave also to them that were with him; which it is not lawful to eat but for the priests alone?
4.5: And it came to pass also on another sabbath, that he entered into the synagogue and taught: and there was a man whose right hand was withered.
4.6: And the scribes and Pharisees watched him, whether he would heal on the sabbath day; that they might find an accusation against him.
4.7: But he knew their reasonings, and said to the man which had the withered hand, Rise up, and stand forth in the midst. And he arose and stood forth.
4.9: Then said Jesus unto them, I will ask you one thing; Is it lawful on the sabbath days to do good, or to do evil? to save life, or to destroy it?
4.10: And looking round about upon them all, he said unto the man, Stretch forth thy hand. And he did so: and his hand was restored whole as the other.
4.11: The same day, Jesus saw a certain man working on the sabbath and said to him: Man, if you know what you are doing, you are blessed. But, if you do not know, you are accursed, and a trespasser of the law.
4.12: And he said unto them, That the Son of man is Lord also of the sabbath.
4.13: And they were filled with madness; and communed one with another what they might do to Jesus.
4.14: And it came to pass in those days, that he went out into a mountain to pray, and continued all night in prayer to God.
4.15: And when it was day, he called unto him his disciples: and of them he chose twelve, whom also he named apostles;
4.16: Simon, whom he also named Peter, and Andrew his brother, James and John, Philip and Bartholomew,
4.17: Matthew and Thomas, James the son of Alphaeus, and Simon called Zelotes,
4.18: And Judas the brother of James, and Judas Iscariot, which also became a traitor.
4.19: And he came down among them, and stood in the plain, and the company of his disciples, and a great multitude of people out of all Judaea and Jerusalem, and from the sea coast of Tyre and Sidon, which came to hear him, and to be healed of their diseases;
4.20: And they that were vexed with unclean spirits: and they were healed.
4.21: And the whole multitude sought to touch him: for there went virtue out of him, and healed them all.
4.22: And he lifted up his eyes on his disciples, and said, Blessed be ye poor: for yours is the kingdom of God.
4.23: Blessed are ye that hunger now: for ye shall be filled. Blessed are ye that weep now: for ye shall laugh.
4.24: Blessed are ye, when men shall hate you, and when they shall separate you from their company, and shall reproach you, and cast out your name as evil, for the Son of man’s sake.
4.25: Rejoice ye in that day, and leap for joy: for, behold, your reward is great in heaven: for in the like manner did their fathers unto the prophets.
4.26: But woe unto you that are rich! for ye have received your consolation.
4.27: Woe unto you that are full! for ye shall hunger. Woe unto you that laugh now! for ye shall mourn and weep.
4.28: Woe unto you, when all men shall speak well of you! for so did their fathers to the false prophets.
4.29: But I say unto you which hear, Love your enemies, do good to them which hate you,
4.30: Bless them that curse you, and pray for them which despitefully use you.
4.31: And unto him that smiteth thee on the one cheek offer also the other; and him that taketh away thy cloak forbid not to take thy coat also.
4.32: Give to every man that asketh of thee; and of him that taketh away thy goods ask them not again.
4.33: And as ye would that men should do to you, do ye also to them likewise.
4.34: For if ye love them which love you, what thank have ye? for sinners also love those that love them.
4.35: And if ye do good to them which do good to you, what thank have ye? for sinners also do even the same.
4.36: And if ye lend to them of whom ye hope to receive, what thank have ye? for sinners also lend to sinners, to receive as much again.
4.37: But love ye your enemies, and do good, and lend, hoping for nothing again; and your reward shall be great, and ye shall be the children of God: for he is kind unto the unthankful and to the evil.
4.38: He maketh his sun to rise on the evil and on the good, and sendeth rain on the just and on the unjust.
4.39: Be ye therefore merciful, as your Father also is merciful.
4.40: Judge not, so that you are not judged; condemn not, so that you are not condemned; forgive, and you shall be forgiven;
4.41: Give, and it shall be given unto you: good measure, pressed down, and running over, shall men give into your bosom. With the same measure that you measure withal, it shall be measured to you again.
4.42: And he spake a parable unto them, Can the blind lead the blind? shall they not both fall into the ditch?
4.43: The disciple is not above his master: but every one that is perfect shall be as his master.
4.44: And why beholdest thou the mote that is in thy brother’s eye, but perceivest not the beam that is in thine own eye?
4.45: Either how canst thou say to thy brother, Brother, let me pull out the mote that is in thine eye, when thou thyself beholdest not the beam that is in thine own eye? Thou hypocrite, cast out first the beam out of thine own eye, and then shalt thou see clearly to pull out the mote that is in thy brother’s eye.
4.46: For there is no good tree that produceth corrupt fruit; nor corrupt tree that produceth good fruit.
4.47: For each tree is known by its own fruit. For of thorns they do not gather figs, nor of a bramble bush gather they grapes.
4.48: The good man out of the good treasure of his heart bringeth forth that which is good; and the evil man out of the evil treasure of his heart bringeth forth that which is evil: for of the abundance of the heart his mouth speaketh.
4.49: Be ye approved money-changers, rejecting some things, but holding fast that which is good.
4.52: He is like a man which built a house, who digged deep, and laid the foundation on a rock: and when the flood arose, the stream beat vehemently upon that house, and could not shake it: for it was founded upon a rock.

5.1: Now when he had ended all his sayings in the audience of the people, he entered into Capernaum.
5.2: And a certain centurion’s servant, who was dear unto him, was sick, and ready to die.
5.3: And when he heard of Jesus, he sent unto him the elders of the Jews, beseeching him that he would come and heal his servant.
5.4: And when they came to Jesus, they besought him instantly, saying, That he was worthy for whom he should do this:
5.5: For he loveth our nation, and he hath built us a synagogue.
5.6: Then Jesus went with them. And when he was now not far from the house, the centurion sent friends to him, saying unto him, Lord, trouble not thyself: for I am not worthy that thou shouldest enter under my roof:
5.7: Wherefore neither thought I myself worthy to come unto thee: but say in a word, and my boy shall be healed.
5.8: For I also am a man set under authority, having under me soldiers, and I say unto one, Go, and he goeth; and to another, Come, and he cometh; and to my servant, Do this, and he doeth it.
5.9: When Jesus heard these things, he marvelled at him, and turned him about, and said unto the people that followed him, I say unto you, I have not found so great faith, no, not in Israel.
5.10: And they that were sent, returning to the house, found the servant whole that had been sick.
5.47: Wherefore I say unto thee, Her sins, which are many, are forgiven; for she loved much: but to whom little is forgiven, the same loveth little.

6.1: And it came to pass afterward, that he went throughout every city and village, preaching and showing the glad tidings of the kingdom of God: and the twelve were with him.
6.2: And certain women, which had been healed of evil spirits and infirmities, Mary called Magdalene, out of whom went seven devils,
6.3: And Joanna the wife of Chuza, Herod’s steward, and Susanna, and many others, which ministered unto him of their substance.
6.4: And when much people were gathered together, and they were come to him out of every city, he spake by a parable:
6.5: A sower went out to sow his seed: and as he sowed, some fell by the way side; and it was trodden down, and the fowls of the heaven devoured it.
6.6: And some fell upon a rock; and as soon as it was sprung up, it withered away, because it lacked moisture.
6.7: And some fell among thorns; and the thorns sprang up with it, and choked it.
6.8: And other fell on the good ground, and sprang up, and bare fruit an hundredfold. And when he said these things, he cried, He that hath ears to hear, let him hear.
6.9: And his disciples asked him, saying, What might this parable be?
6.10: And he said, Unto you it is given to know the mysteries of the kingdom of God: but to others in parables; that seeing they may not see, and hearing they might not understand.
6.11: Now the parable is this: The seed is the word of God.
6.12: Those by the way side are they that hear; then cometh the devil, and taketh away the word out of their hearts, lest they should believe and be saved.
6.13: They on the rock are they, which, when they hear, receive the word with joy; and these have no root, which for a while believe, and in time of temptation fall away.
6.14: And that which fell among thorns are they, which, when they have heard, go forth, and are choked with cares and riches and pleasures of this life, and bring no fruit to perfection.
6.15: No man, when he hath lighted a candle, covereth it with a vessel, or putteth it under a bed; but setteth it on a candlestick, that they which enter in may see the light.
6.16: For nothing is secret, that shall not be made manifest; neither anything hid, that shall not be known and come abroad.
6.17: Take heed therefore how ye hear: for whosoever hath, to him shall be given; and whosoever hath not, even what he seemeth to have shall be taken away.
6.18: To find out if he was birthed, they set a trap for him.
6.19: And it was told him by certain which said, Thy mother and thy brethren stand without, desiring to see thee.
6.20: And he answered and said unto them, Who are my mother and my brethren? None but they who hear my words and do them.
6.21: Now it came to pass on a certain day, that he went into a ship with his disciples: and he said unto them, Let us go over unto the other side of the lake. And they launched forth.
6.22: But as they sailed he fell asleep: and there came down a storm of wind on the lake; and they were filled with water, and were in jeopardy.
6.23: And they came to him, and awoke him, saying, Master, Master, we perish. Then he arose, and rebuked the wind and the raging of the water: and they ceased, and there was a calm.
6.24: And he said unto them, Where is your faith? And they being afraid wondered, saying one to another, What manner of man is this! for he commandeth even the winds and water, and they obey him?
6.25: And they sailed down to the country of the Gadarenes, which is over against Galilee.
6.26: And when he went forth to land, there met him out of the city a certain man, which had devils a long time, and wore no cloke, neither abode in a house, but among the tombs.
6.27: When he saw Jesus, he cried out, and fell down before him, and with a loud voice said, What have I to do with thee, Jesus, thou Son of God? I beseech thee, torment me not.
6.28: For he had commanded the unclean spirit to come out of the man. For oftentimes it had caught him: and he was kept guarded and bound with chains and in fetters; and he brake the bands asunder, and was driven of the devil into the deserts.
6.29: And Jesus asked him, saying, What is thy name? And he said, Legion: because many devils were entered into him.
6.30: And they besought him that he would not command them to go out into the abyss.
6.31: And there was there an herd of many swine feeding on the mountain: and they besought him that he would allow them to enter into them. And he allowed them.
6.32: Then went the devils out of the man, and entered into the swine: and the herd ran violently down the steep place into the lake, and were choked.
6.33: When they that fed them saw what was done, they fled, and went and told it in the city and in the country.
6.34: Then they went out to see what was done; and came to Jesus, and found the man, out of whom the devils were departed, sitting at the feet of Jesus, clothed, and in his right mind: and they were afraid.
6.35: They also which saw it told them by what means he that was possessed of the devils was saved.
6.36: Then the whole multitude of the country of the Gadarenes round about asked him to depart from them; for they were holden with great fear: and he entered into the ship, and returned back again.
6.37: Now the man, from whom the devils were departed, besought him that he might be with him: but Jesus sent him away, saying,
6.38: Return to thine own house, and recount how great things God hath done unto thee. And he went his way, publishing throughout the whole city how great things Jesus had done unto him.
6.39: And it came to pass, that, when Jesus was returned, the people gladly received him: for they were all waiting for him.
6.40: And, behold, there came a man named Jairus, and he was a ruler of the synagogue: and he fell down at Jesus’ feet, and besought him that he would come into his house:
6.41: For he had one only daughter, about twelve years of age, and she was dying.
6.42: And as he went the multitudes thronged him.
6.43: And a woman having an issue of blood twelve years, which had spent all her living upon physicians, neither could be healed of any,
6.44: Came behind him, and touched the border of his garment: and immediately her issue of blood stanched.
6.45: And Jesus said, Who touched me? When all denied, Peter and they that were with him said, Master, the multitude throng thee and press thee, and sayest thou, Who touched me?
6.46: And Jesus said, Somebody hath touched me: for I perceive that virtue is gone out of me.
6.47: And when the woman saw that she was not hid, she came trembling, and falling down before him, she declared unto him before all the people for what cause she had touched him, and how she was healed immediately.
6.48: And he said unto her, Daughter, be of good comfort: thy faith hath saved thee; go in peace.
6.49: While he yet spake, there cometh one from the ruler of the synagogue’s house, saying to him, Thy daughter is dead; trouble not the Master.
6.50: But when Jesus heard it, he answered him, saying, Fear not: believe only, and she shall be saved.
6.51: And when he came into the house, he suffered no man to go in, save Peter, and James, and John, and the father and the mother of the maiden.
6.52: And all were weeping, and bewailed her: but he said, Weep not; she is not dead, but sleepeth.
6.53: And they laughed him to scorn, knowing that she was dead.
6.54: And he put them all out, and took her by the hand, and called, saying, Maid, arise.
6.55: And her spirit came again, and she arose straightway: and he commanded something be given her to eat.
6.56: And her parents were astonished: but he charged them that they should tell no man what was done.

7.1: After this there was a feast of the Jews; and Jesus went up to Jerusalem.
7.2: Now there is at Jerusalem by the sheep market a pool, which is called in the Hebrew tongue Bethesda, having five porches.
7.3: And a certain man was there, which had an infirmity thirty and eight years.
7.4: When Jesus saw him lie, and knew that he had been now a long time in that case, he saith unto him, Wilt thou be made whole?
7.6: Jesus saith unto him, Rise, take up thy bed, and walk.
7.7: And immediately the man was made whole, and took up his bed, and walked: and on the same day was the sabbath.
7.8: The Jews therefore said unto him that was cured, It is the sabbath day: it is not lawful for thee to carry thy bed.
7.9: He answered them, He that made me whole, the same said unto me, Take up thy bed, and walk.
7.10: Then asked they him, What man is that which said unto thee, Take up thy bed, and walk?
7.11: And he that was healed wist not who it was: for Jesus had conveyed himself away, a multitude being in that place.
7.12: Afterward Jesus findeth him in the temple, and said unto him, Behold, thou art made whole: sin no more, lest a worse thing come unto thee.
7.13: The man departed, and told the Jews that it was Jesus, which had made him whole.
7.14: And therefore did the Jews persecute Jesus, and sought to slay him, because he had done these things on the sabbath day.
7.15: But Jesus answered them, My Father worketh hitherto, and I work.
7.16: Therefore the Jews sought the more to kill him, because he not only had broken the sabbath, but said also that God was his Father, making himself equal with God.
7.17: Then answered Jesus and said unto them, Verily, verily, I say unto you, The Son can do nothing of himself, but what he seeth the Father do: for what things soever he doeth, these also doeth the Son likewise.
7.18: For the Father loveth the Son, and sheweth him all things that himself doeth: and he will shew him greater works than these, that ye may marvel.
7.19: For as the Father raiseth up the dead, and quickeneth them; even so the Son quickeneth whom he will.
7.20: For the Father judgeth no man, but hath committed all judgment unto the Son:
7.21: That all men should honour the Son, even as they honour the Father. He that honoureth not the Son honoureth not the Father which hath sent him.
7.22: Verily, verily, I say unto you, He that heareth my word, and believeth on him that sent me, hath everlasting life, and shall not come into condemnation; but is passed from death unto life.
7.23: Verily, verily, I say unto you, The hour is coming, and now is, when the dead shall hear the voice of the Son of God: and they that hear shall live.
7.24: For as the Father hath life in himself; so hath he given to the Son to have life in himself;
7.25: And hath given him authority to execute judgment also, because he is the Son of man.
7.26: Marvel not at this: for the hour is coming, in the which all that are in the graves shall hear his voice,
7.27: And shall come forth; they that have done good, unto the resurrection of life; and they that have done evil, unto the resurrection of damnation.
7.28: I can of mine own self do nothing: as I hear, I judge: and my judgment is just; because I seek not mine own will, but the will of the Father which hath sent me.
7.29: If I bear witness of myself, my witness is not true.
7.30: There is another that beareth witness of me; and I know that the witness which he witnesseth of me is true.
7.31: Ye sent unto John, and he bare witness unto the truth.
7.32: But I receive not testimony from man: but these things I say, that ye might be saved.
7.33: He was a burning and a shining light: and ye were willing for a season to rejoice in his light.
7.34: But I have greater witness than that of John: for the works which the Father hath given me to finish, the same works that I do, bear witness of me, that the Father hath sent me.
7.35: And the Father himself, which hath sent me, hath borne witness of me. Ye have neither heard his voice at any time, nor seen his shape.
7.36: And ye have not his word abiding in you: for whom he hath sent, him ye believe not.

8.1: Then he called his twelve disciples together, and gave them power and authority over all the devils, and to cure diseases.
8.2: And he sent them to preach the kingdom of God, and to heal the sick.
8.3: And he said unto them, Take nothing for your journey, neither staves, nor scrip, neither bread, neither money; neither have two coats apiece, for the worker is worthy of his hire.
8.4: And whatsoever house ye enter into, there abide, and thence depart.
8.5: And whosoever will not receive you, when ye go out of that city, shake off the very dust from your feet for a testimony against them.
8.6: And they departed, and went through the villages in order, announcing the glad tidings, and healing every where.
8.7: Now Herod the tetrarch heard of all that was done by him: and he was perplexed, because that it was said of some, that John was risen from the dead;
8.8: And of some, that Elias had appeared; and by others, that a prophet, one of the original ones, was risen again.
8.9: And Herod said, John have I beheaded: but who is this, of whom I hear such things? And he desired to see him.
8.10: For Herod himself had sent forth and laid hold upon John, and bound him in prison for Herodias’ sake, his brother Philip’s wife: for he had married her.
8.11: For John had said unto Herod, It is not lawful for thee to have thy brother’s wife.
8.12: Therefore Herodias had a quarrel against him, and would have killed him; but she could not:
8.13: For Herod feared John, knowing that he was a just man and an holy, and observed him; and when he heard him, he did many things, and heard him gladly.
8.14: And when a convenient day was come, that Herod on his birthday made a supper to his lords, high captains, and chief estates of Galilee;
8.15: And when the daughter of the said Herodias came in, and danced, and pleased Herod and them that sat with him, the king said unto the damsel, Ask of me whatsoever thou wilt, and I will give it thee.
8.16: And he sware unto her, Whatsoever thou shalt ask of me, I will give it thee, unto the half of my kingdom.
8.17: And she went forth, and said unto her mother, What shall I ask? And she said, The head of John the Baptist.
8.18: And she came in straightway with haste unto the king, and asked, saying, I will that thou give me by and by in a charger the head of John the Baptist.
8.19: And the king was exceeding sorry; yet for his oath’s sake, and for their sakes which sat with him, he would not reject her.
8.20: And immediately the king sent an executioner, and commanded his head to be brought: and he went and beheaded him in the prison,
8.21: And brought his head in a charger, and gave it to the damsel: and the damsel gave it to her mother.
8.22: And when his disciples heard of it, they came and took up his corpse, and laid it in a tomb.
8.23: And the apostles returned, and declared unto him all that they had done. And he took them, and went aside privately into a desert place of a city called Bethsaida.
8.24: And the multitudes perceived it, and followed him: and he received them, and spake unto them of the kingdom of God, and healed them that had need of healing.
8.25: And when the day began to decline, and the twelve came, and said unto him, Send the multitude away, that they may go into the towns and country round about, and lodge, and fetch victuals: for we are in a desert place.
8.26: But he said unto them, Give ye them to eat. And they said, We have no more but five loaves and two fishes; except we should go and buy food for all this people.
8.27: For they were about five thousand men. And he said to his disciples, Make them recline in companies by fifties.
8.28: And they did so, and made them all recline.
8.29: And he took the five loaves and the two fishes, and looking up to heaven, he blessed them, and brake, and gave to the disciples to set before the multitude.
8.30: And they did eat, and were all filled: and there was taken up that remained to them of fragments twelve baskets.
8.31: Then Jesus said unto them, Verily, verily, I say unto you, Moses gave you not that bread from heaven; but my Father giveth you the true bread from heaven.
8.32: For the bread of God is he which cometh down from heaven, and giveth life unto the world.
8.33: Then said they unto him, Lord, evermore give us this bread.
8.35: And Jesus said unto them, I am the bread of life: he that cometh to me shall never hunger; and he that believeth on me shall never thirst
8.36: And straightway he constrained his disciples to get into the ship, and to go to the other side before unto Bethsaida, while he sent away the people.
8.37: And when he had sent them away, he departed into a mountain to pray.
8.38: And when even was come, the ship was in the midst of the sea, and he alone on the land.
8.39: And he saw them toiling in rowing; for the wind was contrary unto them: and about the fourth watch of the night he cometh unto them, walking upon the sea, and would have passed by them.
8.40: But when they saw him walking upon the sea, they supposed it had been a spirit, and cried out:
8.41: For they all saw him, and were troubled. And immediately he talked with them, and saith unto them, Be of good cheer: it is I; be not afraid.
8.42: And he went up unto them into the ship; and the wind ceased: and they were sore amazed in themselves beyond measure, and wondered.
8.43: For they considered not the miracle of the loaves: for their heart was hardened.
8.44: Then Jesus went thence, and departed into the coasts of Tyre and Sidon.
8.45: And, behold, a woman of Canaan came out of the same coasts, and cried unto him, saying, Have mercy on me, O Lord, thou son of David; my daughter is grievously vexed with a devil.
8.46: But he answered her not a word. And his disciples came and besought him, saying, Send her away; for she crieth after us.
8.47: Then came she and worshipped him, saying, Lord, help me.
8.48: Then Jesus answered and said unto her, O woman, great is thy faith: be it unto thee even as thou wilt. And her daughter was made whole from that very hour.
8.49: And it came to pass, as he was alone praying, his disciples were with him: and he asked them, saying, Whom say the people that I am?
8.50: They answering said, John the Baptist; but some say, Elias; and others say, that one some prophet of the original ones is risen again.
8.51: He said unto them, But whom say ye that I am? Peter answering said, The Christ of God.
8.52: And he straightly reprimanded them, and commanded them to tell none such a thing;
8.53: Saying, The Son of man must suffer many things, and be rejected of the elders and chief priests and scribes, and be slain, and be raised after three days.
8.54: And he said to them all, If any man will come after me, let him deny himself, and take up his cross daily, and follow me.
8.55: For whosoever wishes to save his life shall lose it: but whosoever shall lose his life for my sake, the same shall save it.
8.56: For what is a man profited, if he gain the whole world, and lose or forfeit his own self?
8.57: For whosoever shall be ashamed of me, of him shall the Son of man be ashamed when he shall come in his own glory, and in the glory of his Father’s and of the holy angels.
8.58: But I tell you truly, there be some of those that stand here, which shall by no means taste death, till they see the kingdom of God.
8.59: And it came to pass about an eight days after these sayings, he took Peter and John and James, and went up into a mountain to pray.
8.60: And as he prayed, the fashion of his countenance was altered, and his raiment was white, and glistering.
8.61: And, behold, there talked with him two men, which Peter believed were Moses and Elias,
8.62: Who appeared in glory, and spake of his decease, which he should accomplish at Jerusalem.
8.63: But Peter and they that were with him were heavy with sleep: but when they were awake, they saw his glory, and the two men that stood with him.
8.64: And it came to pass, as they departed from him, Peter said unto Jesus, Teacher, it is good for us to be here: and let us make three tabernacles; one for thee, and one for Moses, and one for Elias: not knowing what he said.
8.65: While he thus spake, there came a cloud, and overshadowed them: and they feared as they entered into the cloud.
8.66: And a voice came out of the cloud, saying, This is my beloved Son: hear him.
8.67: And when the voice was past, Jesus was found alone. And they kept in close, and told no man in those days any of those things which they had seen.
8.68: And it came to pass, that on the next day, when they were come down from the mountain, a great multitude met him.
8.69: And they were all amazed at the majesty of God. And as they all wondered at all things which Jesus did, he said unto his disciples,
8.70: And, lo, a spirit taketh him, and he suddenly crieth out; and it convulseth him with foam, and bruising him hardly departeth from him.
8.71: And I besought thy disciples to cast him out; and they could not.
8.72: And Jesus answering said, O faithless and perverse generation, how long shall I be with you, and suffer you? Bring thy son hither.
8.73: And as he was yet a coming, the devil rent him, and convulsed him. And Jesus rebuked the unclean spirit, and healed the boy, and delivered him again to his father.
8.74: Take ye into your ears these sayings: for the Son of man is going to be delivered into the hands of men.
8.75: But they understood not this saying, and it was hid from them that they should not perceive it: and they feared to ask him about that saying.
8.76: Then there arose a reasoning among them, which of them should be greatest.
8.77: And Jesus, perceiving the reasoning of their heart, took a child, and set it next to himself, and said unto them,
8.78: Whosoever shall receive this child in my name receiveth me: and whosoever shall receive me receiveth him that sent me: for he that is least among you all, the same shall be great.
8.79: And John answered and said, Teacher, we saw one casting out devils in thy name; and we forbad him, because he followeth not with us.
8.80: And Jesus said unto him, Forbid him not: for he that is not against us is for us.
8.81: And it came to pass, when the time was come that he should be received up, he stedfastly set his face to go to Jerusalem,
8.82: And sent messengers before his face: and they went, and entered into a village of the Samaritans, so as to make ready for him.
8.83: And they did not receive him, because his face was going to Jerusalem.
8.84: And when his disciples James and John saw this, they said, Lord, wilt thou that we command fire to come down from heaven, and to consume them, even as Elias did?
8.85: But he turned, and rebuked them, and said, Ye know not what manner of spirit ye are of.
8.86: For the Son of man came not to destroy men’s lives, but to save them. And they went to another village.
8.87: And it came to pass, that, as they went in the way, a certain man said unto him, Lord, I will follow thee whithersoever thou goest.
8.88: And Jesus said unto him, Foxes have holes, and the birds of the air have nests; but the Son of man hath not where to lay his head.
8.89: And he said unto another, Follow me. But he said, Lord, suffer me first to go and bury my father.
8.90: Jesus said unto him, Let the dead to bury their dead: but go thou and preach the kingdom of God.
8.91: And another also said, Lord, I will follow thee; but let me first go bid them farewell, which are at home at my house.
8.92: And Jesus said unto him, No man, having put his hand to the plough, and looking back, is fit for the kingdom of God.

9.1: And after these things the Lord appointed other seventy also, and sent them two and two before his face into every city and place, whither he himself was about to come.
9.2: Therefore said he unto them, The harvest truly is great, but the labourers are few: pray ye therefore the Lord of the harvest, that he may send forth labourers into his harvest.
9.3: Go your ways: behold, I send you forth as lambs among wolves.
9.4: Carry neither purse, nor wallet, nor shoes: and salute no man by the way.
9.5: And into whatsoever house ye enter, first say, Peace be to this house.
9.6: And if the son of peace be there, your peace shall rest upon it: if not, it shall turn to you again.
9.7: And in the same house remain, eating and drinking such things as they give: for the labourer is worthy of his hire. Go not from house to house.
9.8: And into whatsoever city ye enter, and they receive you, eat such things as are set before you:
9.9: And heal the sick that are therein, and say unto them, The kingdom of God is come nigh unto you.
9.10: But into whatsoever city ye enter, and they receive you not, go out into the streets thereof, and say,
9.11: Even the very dust of your city, which cleaveth on us, we do wipe off against you: notwithstanding be ye sure of this, that the kingdom of God is come nigh unto you.
9.12: But I say unto you, that it shall be more tolerable in that day for Sodom, than for that city.
9.13: Woe unto thee, Chorazin! woe unto thee, Bethsaida! for if the powers had been done in Tyre and Sidon, which have been done in you, they would have repented long ago, sitting in sackcloth and ashes.
9.14: But it shall be more tolerable for Tyre and Sidon at the judgment, than for you.
9.15: And thou, Capernaum, which art exalted to heaven? thou shalt be thrust down unto hell.
9.16: He that heareth you heareth me; and he that despiseth you despiseth me; and he that despiseth me despiseth him that sent me.
9.17: And the seventy returned again with joy, saying, Lord, even the devils are subject unto us through thy name.
9.18: And he said unto them, I beheld Satan as lightning fallen from heaven.
9.19: Behold, I give unto you authority to tread on serpents and scorpions, and over all the power of the enemy: and nothing shall by any means hurt you.
9.20: Notwithstanding, in this rejoice not, that the spirits are subject unto you; but rather rejoice, because your names are written in the heavens.
9.21: In that hour Jesus rejoiced in spirit, and said, I thank thee, Lord of heaven, that thou hast hid these things from the wise and prudent, and hast revealed them unto babes: even so, Father; for so it was well-pleasing before thee.
9.22: All things were delivered to me by my Father: and no man knoweth who the Father is, but the Son, and who the Son is, but the Father, and he to whomsoever the Son wishes to reveal him.
9.23: Come unto me, all ye that labour and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest.
9.24: Take my yoke upon you, and learn of me; for I am meek and lowly in heart: and ye shall find rest unto your souls.
9.25: For my yoke is easy, and my burden is light.
9.26: And he turned him unto his disciples, and said privately, Blessed are the eyes which see the things that ye see:
9.27: For I tell you, that prophets have not seen the things which ye see; and to hear those things which ye hear, and have not heard them.
9.28: And, behold, a certain lawyer stood up, and tempted him, saying, Teacher, doing what shall I obtain life?
9.29: He said unto him, What is written in the law? how readest thou?
9.30: And he answering said, Thou shalt love the Lord thy God with all thy heart, and with all thy soul, and with all thy strength.
9.31: And he said unto him, Thou hast answered right: this do, and thou shalt live.
9.32: But he, willing to justify himself, said unto Jesus, And who is my neighbour?
9.33: And Jesus answering said, A certain man was going down from Jerusalem to Jericho, and fell among thieves, which stripped him of his raiment, and wounded him, and departed, leaving him half dead.
9.34: And by chance there came down a certain priest in that way: and when he saw him, he passed by on the other side.
9.35: And likewise a Levite, when he was at the place, came and looked on him, and passed by on the other side.
9.36: But a certain Samaritan, as he journeyed, came where he was: and when he saw him, he had compassion on him,
9.37: And went to him, and bound up his wounds, pouring in oil and wine, and set him on his own beast, and brought him to an inn, and took care of him.
9.38: And on the morrow when he departed, he took out two pence, and gave them to the host, and said unto him, Take care of him; and whatsoever thou spendest more, when I come again, I will repay thee.
9.39: Which now of these three, thinkest thou, was neighbour unto him that fell among the thieves?
9.40: And he said, He that showed mercy on him. Then said Jesus unto him, Go, and do thou likewise.
9.41: Now it came to pass, as they went, that he entered into a certain village: and a certain woman named Martha received him into her house.
9.42: And she had a sister called Mary, which also sat at Jesus’ feet, and heard his word.
9.43: But Martha was cumbered about much serving, and came to him, and said, Lord, dost thou not care that my sister hath left me to serve alone? bid her therefore that she help me.
9.44: And Jesus answered and said unto her, Martha, Martha, thou art careful and troubled about many things:
9.45: But one thing is needful: and Mary hath chosen that good part, which shall not be taken away from her.

10.1: And it came to pass that, as he was praying in a certain place, when he ceased, one of his disciples said unto him, Lord, teach us to pray, as John also taught his disciples.
10.2: And he said unto them, When ye pray, say,
10.5: And I say unto you, Ask, and it shall be given you; seek, and ye shall find; knock, and it shall be opened unto you.
10.6: For every one that asketh receiveth; and he that seeketh findeth; and to him that knocketh it shall be opened.
10.7: And he said unto them, Which of you shall have a friend, and shall go unto him at midnight, and say unto him, Friend, lend me three loaves;
10.8: Since my friend is come unto me from a journey, and I have nothing to set before him?
10.9: And he from within shall answer and say, Trouble me not: the door is now shut, and my children are with me in bed; I cannot rise and give thee.
10.10: I say unto you, even if he will not rise and give him, because he is his friend, yet because of his importunity he will rise and give him as many as he needeth.
10.11: If a son shall ask bread of any of you that is a father, will he give him a stone? or if he ask a fish, will he for a fish give him a serpent?
10.12: Or if he shall ask an egg, will he offer him a scorpion?
10.13: If ye then, being evil, know how to give good gifts unto your children: how much more shall your heavenly Father give the Holy Spirit to them that ask him?
10.14: And he was casting out a devil, and it was dumb. And it came to pass, when the devil was gone out, the dumb spake; and the multitudes marvelled.
10.15: But some of them said, He casteth out devils through Beelzebub the chief of the devils.
10.16: And others, tempting him, sought of him a sign from heaven.
10.17: But he, knowing their thoughts, said unto them, Every kingdom divided against itself is brought to desolation; and a house divided against a house falleth.
10.18: And if Satan also be divided against himself, how shall his kingdom stand? because ye say that I cast out devils in Beelzebub.
10.19: And if I in Beelzebub cast out devils, in whom do your sons cast them out? therefore shall they be your judges.
10.20: But if I with the finger of God cast out devils, no doubt the kingdom of God is come upon you.
10.21: When a strong man fully armed keepeth his palace, his goods are in peace.
10.22: But when the stronger than he shall come upon him, and overcome him, he taketh from him all his whole armour wherein he trusted, and divideth his spoils.
10.23: He that is not with me is against me: and he that gathereth not with me scattereth.
10.24: When the unclean spirit is gone out of a man, he walketh through dry places, seeking rest; and finding none, he saith, I will return into my house whence I came out.
10.25: And when he cometh, he findeth it swept and garnished.
10.26: Then goeth he, and taketh to him seven other spirits more wicked than himself; and they enter in, and dwell there: and the last state of that man is worse than the first.
10.27: And it came to pass, as he said these things, a certain woman out of the multitude lifted up her voice, and said unto him, Blessed is the womb that bare thee, and the breasts which thou didst suck.
10.28: But he said, Yea rather, blessed are they that hear the word of God, and keep it.
10.29: And when the people were gathered thick together, he began to say, This generation: they seek a sign; and there shall no sign be given it.
10.30: No man, when he hath lighted a lamp, putteth it into a secret place, neither under a bushel, but on the lampstand, that they which come in may see the light.
10.31: The lamp of the body is the eye: therefore when thine eye is single, thy whole body also is full of light; but when it is evil, thy body also is full of darkness.
10.32: Look therefore that the light which is in thee be not darkness.
10.33: If therefore thy whole body be full of light, having no part dark, it shall be wholly full of light, as when the lamp with its gleam doth give thee light.
10.34: And as he spake, a certain Pharisee besought him to dine with him: and he went in, and sat down to meat.
10.35: And when the Pharisee saw it, he marvelled that he had not first washed before dinner.
10.36: And the Lord said unto him, Now do ye Pharisees make clean the outside of the cup and the platter; but your inward part is full of ravening and wickedness.
10.37: Foolish ones, did not he that made the outside make the inside also?
10.38: But give as alms the things in your power; and, behold, all things are clean unto you.
10.39: But woe unto you, Pharisees! for ye tithe mint and rue and every herb, and pass over the calling and the love of God: but these ought ye to have done, and not to leave the other undone.
10.40: Woe unto you, Pharisees! for ye love the uppermost seats in the synagogues, and the greetings in the markets.
10.41: Woe unto you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! for ye are as graves which appear not, and the men that walk about over them know it not.
10.42: Then answered one of the lawyers, and said unto him, Master, thus saying thou reproachest us also.
10.43: And he said, Woe unto you also, lawyers! for ye lade men with burdens grievous to be borne, and ye yourselves touch not the burdens with one of your fingers.
10.44: Woe unto you! for ye build the sepulchres of the prophets, but your fathers killed them.
10.45: Truly ye are witnesses and consent to the deeds of your fathers: for they indeed killed them, but ye build their sepulchres.
10.46: Woe unto you, lawyers! for ye took away the key of knowledge: ye entered not in yourselves, and them that were entering in ye hindered.
10.47: And as he said these things unto them, the scribes and the Pharisees began to urge him vehemently, and to provoke him to speak of many things:
10.48: Laying wait for him, and seeking to catch something out of his mouth, that they might accuse him.

11.1: In the mean time, when there were gathered together an innumerable multitude of people, insomuch that they trode one upon another, he began to say unto his disciples first of all, Beware ye of the leaven of the Pharisees, which is hypocrisy.
11.2: For there is nothing covered up, that shall not be revealed; and hidden that shall not be known.
11.3: Therefore whatsoever ye have spoken in darkness shall be heard in the light; and that which ye have spoken to the ear in the chambers shall be proclaimed upon the housetops.
11.4: And I say unto you my friends, Be not afraid of them that kill the body, and after that have no more that they can do.
11.5: But I will forewarn you whom ye shall fear: Fear him, which after he hath killed hath power to cast into hell; yea, I say unto you, Fear him.
11.6: But even the very hairs of your head are all numbered. Fear not therefore: ye are of more value than many sparrows.
11.7: Also I say unto you, Whosoever shall confess me before men, him shall I also confess before God:
11.8: But he that denieth me before men shall be denied before God.
11.9: And every one, who shall speak against the Son of man, it shall be forgiven him: but unto him that speak against the Holy Spirit it shall not be forgiven.
11.10: And when they bring you unto the synagogues, and unto magistrates, and powers, take ye no thought how or what thing ye shall answer, or what ye shall say:
11.11: For the Holy Spirit shall teach you in the same hour what ye ought to say.
11.12: And one out of the company said unto him, Master, speak to my brother, that he divide the inheritance with me.
11.13: And he said unto him, Man, who made me a judge or a divider over you?
11.14: And he said unto them, Take heed, and beware of covetousness: for a man’s life consisteth not in the abundance of the things which he possesseth.
11.15: And he spake a parable unto them, saying, The ground of a certain rich man brought forth plentifully:
11.16: And he reasoned within himself, saying, What shall I do, because I have not where to collect my fruits?
11.17: And he said, This will I do: I will pull down my barns, and build greater; and there will I collect all my fruits and my goods.
11.18: And I will say to my soul, Soul, thou hast much goods laid up for many years; take thine ease, eat, drink, and be merry.
11.19: But God said unto him, Foolish one, this night they require thy soul from thee: and the things which thou hast prepared, whose shall they be?
11.20: So is he that layeth up treasure for himself, and is not rich toward God.
11.21: And he said unto his disciples, Therefore I say unto you, Be not anxious for your life, what ye shall eat; neither for the body, what ye shall put on.
11.22: The life is more than the food, and the body than the raiment.
11.23: Consider the ravens, that they sow not nor reap; which have not storechamber nor barn; and God feedeth them: how much rather do ye excel the birds!
11.24: And which of you with being anxious can add to his stature one cubit?
11.25: If ye then be not able to do even a very little thing, why are ye anxious about the rest?
11.26: Consider the lilies and grass: they toil not, nor spin, and yet are clothed. I say unto you, that Solomon in all his glory was not arrayed like one of these;
11.27: If then God so clothe the grass, which is today in the field, and tomorrow is cast into the oven; how much more will he clothe you, O ye of little faith?
11.28: And seek not ye what ye shall eat, or what ye shall drink, neither be ye of doubtful mind.
11.29: For all these things do the nations of the world seek after: and your Father knoweth that ye have need of these things.
11.30: But seek ye the kingdom of God; and all these things shall be added unto you.
11.31: Fear not, little flock; for it is Father’s good pleasure to give you the kingdom.
11.32: Sell your possessions, and give alms; make yourselves purses which grow not old, an unfailing treasure in the heavens, where a thief does not come near, nor a moth destroy.
11.33: For where your treasure is, there will your heart be also.
11.34: Let your loins be girded about, and your lamps burning;
11.35: And ye like unto men that wait for their lord, when he shall return from the wedding-feast; that when he cometh and knocketh, they may open unto him immediately.
11.36: Blessed are those servants, whom the lord when he cometh shall find watching: verily I say unto you, that he shall gird himself, and make them to recline to meat, and shall pass by and serve them.
11.37: And if he shall come in the evening watch, and find them so, blessed are those servants.
11.38: And this know, that if the master of the house had known what hour the thief was coming, he would not have suffered his house to be broken through.
11.39: Be ye therefore ready also: for the Son of man cometh at an hour when ye think not.
11.40: Then Peter said unto him, Lord, speakest thou this parable unto us, or even unto all?
11.41: And the Lord said, Who then is that faithful and wise steward, whom his lord shall set over his household, to give them their portion of food in due season?
11.42: Blessed is that servant, whom his lord when he cometh shall find so doing.
11.43: Of a truth I say unto you, that he will set him over all his possessions.
11.44: But if that servant shall say in his heart, My lord delayeth to come, and shall begin to beat the menservants and maidservants, and to eat and drink, and to be drunken;
11.45: The lord of that servant will come in a day when he expecteth not, and at an hour when he knoweth not, and will cut him in sunder, and will appoint him his portion with the unfaithful.
11.46: And that servant, which knew his lord’s will, and prepared not, neither did according to his will, shall be beaten with many stripes.
11.47: But he that knew not, and did things worthy of stripes, shall be beaten with few stripes. For to whomsoever much is given, of him shall much be required: and to whom they have committed much, of him they will ask the more.
11.48: Then shall the kingdom of heaven be likened unto ten virgins, which took their lamps, and went forth to meet the bridegroom.
11.49: And five of them were wise, and five were foolish.
11.50: They that were foolish took their lamps, and took no oil with them:
11.51: But the wise took oil in their vessels with their lamps.
11.52: While the bridegroom tarried, they all slumbered and slept.
11.53: And at midnight there was a cry made, Behold, the bridegroom cometh; go ye out to meet him.
11.54: Then all those virgins arose, and trimmed their lamps.
11.55: And the foolish said unto the wise, Give us of your oil; for our lamps are gone out.
11.56: But the wise answered, saying, Not so; lest there be not enough for us and you: but go ye rather to them that sell, and buy for yourselves.
11.57: And while they went to buy, the bridegroom came; and they that were ready went in with him to the marriage: and the door was shut.
11.58: Afterward came also the other virgins, saying, Lord, Lord, open to us.
11.59: But he answered and said, Verily I say unto you, I know you not.
11.60: Watch therefore, for ye know neither the day nor the hour wherein the Son of man cometh.
11.61: I came to cast fire on the earth; and what will I, if it be already kindled?
11.62: But I have a baptism to be baptized with; and how am I constrained till it be accomplished!
11.63: Think ye that I am come to give peace on earth? I tell you, Nay; but rather division:
11.64: For from henceforth there shall be five in one house divided, three against two, and two against three.
11.65: The father shall be divided against the son, and the son against father; the mother against the daughter, and the daughter against the mother; the mother-in-law against her daughter-in-law, and the daughter-in-law against her mother-in-law.
11.66: And he said also to the multitudes, When ye see the cloud rising up from the west, straightway ye say There cometh a shower; and it cometh to pass.
11.67: And when ye see a south wind blowing, ye say, There will be scorching heat; and it cometh to pass.
11.68: Ye hypocrites, ye know how to interpret the face of the earth and the heaven; but how is it that ye do not know how to interpret this time?
11.69: Yea, and why even of yourselves judge ye not what is right?
11.70: For as thou goest with thine adversary before the magistrate, on the way give diligence to be delivered from him; lest he hale thee to the judge, and the judge deliver thee to the officer, and the officer cast thee into prison.
11.71: I say unto thee, thou shalt by no means depart thence, till thou hast paid the very last lepton.

12.1: He spake also this parable; A certain man had a fig tree planted in his vineyard; and he came and sought fruit thereon, and found none.
12.2: Then said he unto the dresser of his vineyard, Behold, these three years I come seeking fruit on this fig tree, and find none: cut it down: why cumbereth it the ground?
12.3: And he answering said unto him, Lord, let it alone this year also, till I shall dig about it, and dung it:
12.4: And if it bear fruit, well: and if not, then after that thou shalt cut it down.
12.5: And he was teaching in one of the synagogues on the sabbath days.
12.6: And, behold, there was a woman which had a spirit of infirmity eighteen years, and was bowed together, and could in no wise lift up herself.
12.7: And when Jesus saw her, he called her to him, and said unto her, Woman, thou art loosed from thine infirmity.
12.8: And he laid his hands on her: and immediately she was made straight, and glorified God.
12.9: And the ruler of the synagogue answered, being vexed, because that Jesus had healed on the sabbath day, and said unto the multitude, There are six days in which men ought to work: in them therefore come and be healed, and not on the day of the sabbath.
12.10: The Lord then answered him, and said, Thou hypocrite, doth not each one of you on the sabbath loose his ox or his ass from the stall, and lead him away to watering?
12.11: And ought not this woman, being a daughter of Abraham, whom Satan hath bound, lo, these eighteen years, be loosed from this bond on the day of the sabbath?
12.12: And when he had said these things, all his adversaries were put to shame: and all the multitude rejoiced for all the glorious things that were done by him.
12.13: Another parable put he forth unto them, saying, The kingdom of heaven is likened unto a man which sowed good seed in his field:
12.14: But while men slept, his enemy came and sowed tares among the wheat, and went his way.
12.15: But when the blade was sprung up, and brought forth fruit, then appeared the tares also.
12.16: So the servants of the householder came and said unto him, Sir, didst not thou sow good seed in thy field? from whence then hath it tares?
12.17: He said unto them, An enemy hath done this. The servants said unto him, Wilt thou then that we go and gather them up?
12.18: But he said, Nay; lest while ye gather up the tares, ye root up also the wheat with them.
12.19: Let both grow together until the harvest: and in the time of harvest I will say to the reapers, Gather ye together first the tares, and bind them in bundles to burn them: but gather the wheat into my barn.
12.20: Again, the kingdom of heaven is like unto treasure hid in a field; the which when a man hath found, he hideth, and for joy thereof goeth and selleth all that he hath, and buyeth that field.
12.21: Again, the kingdom of heaven is like unto a merchant man, seeking goodly pearls:
12.22: Who, when he had found one pearl of great price, went and sold all that he had, and bought it.
12.23: Then said he, Unto what is the kingdom of God like? and whereunto shall I liken it?
12.24: It is like a grain of mustard seed, which a man took, and cast into his garden; and it grew, and waved a great tree; and the fowls of the air lodged in the branches of it.
12.25: And again he said, Whereunto shall I liken the kingdom of God?
12.26: It is like unto leaven, which a woman took and hid in three measures of meal, till the whole was leavened.
12.27: Then Jesus sent the multitude away, and went into the house: and his disciples came unto him, saying, Declare unto us the parable of the tares of the field.
12.28: He answered and said unto them, He that soweth the good seed is the Son of man;
12.29: The field is the world; the good seed are the children of the kingdom; but the tares are the children of the wicked one;
12.30: The enemy that sowed them is the devil; the harvest is the end of the world; and the reapers are the angels.
12.31: As therefore the tares are gathered and burned in the fire; so shall it be in the end of this world.
12.32: The Son of man shall send forth his angels, and they shall gather out of his kingdom all things that offend, and them which do iniquity;
12.33: And shall cast them into a furnace of fire: there shall be wailing and gnashing of teeth.
12.34: Then shall the righteous shine forth as the sun in the kingdom of their Father. Who hath ears to hear, let him hear.
12.35: Again, the kingdom of heaven is like unto a net, that was cast into the sea, and gathered of every kind:
12.36: Which, when it was full, they drew to shore, and sat down, and gathered the good into vessels, but cast the bad away.
12.37: So shall it be at the end of the world: the angels shall come forth, and sever the wicked from among the just,
12.38: And shall cast them into the furnace of fire: there shall be wailing and gnashing of teeth.
12.39: And he went through the cities and villages, teaching, and making a journey unto Jerusalem.
12.40: Then one said unto him, Lord, are they few that are being saved? And he said unto them,
12.41: Strive to enter in through the narrow gate: for many, I say unto you, will seek to enter in, and shall not be able.
12.42: When once the master of the house is risen up, and hath shut to the door, and ye begin to stand outside, and to knock at the door, saying, Lord, Lord, open unto us; and he shall answer and say unto you, I know you not whence ye are:
12.43: Then shall ye begin to say, We did eat and drink in thy presence, and thou didst teach in our streets.
12.44: But he shall say, I tell you, I know you not whence ye are; depart from me, all ye workers of unrighteousness.
12.45: When ye see all the righteous in the kingdom of God, and you yourselves kept out, there shall be weeping and gnashing of teeth.

13.1: And it came to pass, as he went into the house of one of the chief Pharisees to eat bread on the sabbath day, that they watched him.
13.2: And, behold, there was a certain man before him which had the dropsy.
13.3: And Jesus answering spake unto the lawyers and Pharisees, saying, Is it lawful to heal on the sabbath day?
13.4: And they held their peace. And he took him, and healed him, and let him go;
13.5: And answered them, saying, Which of you shall have an ass or an ox fallen into a pit, and will not straightway pull him out on the sabbath day?
13.6: And they could not answer him again to these things.
13.7: And he put forth a parable to those which were bidden, when he marked how they chose out the chief rooms; saying unto them,
13.8: When thou art bidden of any man to a wedding, sit not down in the highest room; lest a more honorable man thou be bidden of him;
13.9: And he that bade thee and him come and say to thee, Give this man place; and thou begin with shame to take the lowest room.
13.10: But when thou art bidden, go and sit down in the lowest room; that when he that bade thee cometh, he may say unto thee, friend, go up higher: then shalt thou have worship in the presence of them that sit at meat with thee.
13.11: For whosoever exalteth himself shall be abased; and he that humbleth himself shall be exalted.
13.12: Then said he also to him that bade him, When thou makest a breakfast or a supper, call not thy friends, nor thy brethren, neither thy kinsmen, nor thy rich neighbours; lest they also bid thee again, and a recompence be made thee.
13.13: But when thou makest a feast, call the poor, the maimed, the lame, the blind:
13.14: And thou shalt be blessed; for they have nothing wherewith to recompense thee: for it shall be recompensed to thee at the resurrection of the righteous.
13.15: And when one of them that reclined with him heard these things, he said unto him, Blessed is he that shall eat bread in the kingdom of God.
13.16: But he said unto him, A certain man made a great supper, and bade many:
13.17: And sent his servant at supper time to say to them that were bidden, Come; for all things are now ready.
13.18: And they all with one consent began to make excuse. The first said unto him, I have bought a piece of ground, and I must needs go and see it: I pray thee have me excused.
13.19: And another said, I have bought five yoke of oxen, and I go to prove them: I pray thee, hold me excused.
13.20: And another said, I have married a wife, and therefore I cannot come.
13.21: So that servant came, and shewed his lord these things. Then the master of the house being moved said to his servant, Go out quickly into the streets and lanes of the city, and bring in hither the poor, and the maimed, and the halt, and the blind.
13.22: And the servant said, Lord, it is done as thou didst command, and yet there is room.
13.23: And the lord said unto the servant, Go out into the highways and hedges, and compel them to come in, that my house may be filled.
13.24: For I say unto you, That none of those men which were bidden shall taste of my supper.
13.25: And there went great multitudes with him: and he turned, and said unto them,
13.26: If any man cometh to me, and doth not abandon his father, and mother, and wife, and children, and brethren, and sisters, yea, and his own life also, he cannot be my disciple.
13.27: And whosoever doth not bear his cross, and come behind me, cannot be my disciple.
13.28: For which of you, intending to build a tower, sitteth not down first, and counteth the cost, whether he have sufficient to finish it?
13.29: Lest haply, after he hath laid the foundation, and is not able to finish, all that behold it begin to mock him,
13.30: Saying, This man began to build, and was not able to finish.
