20.4: He who gave me authority over bonds that I might loose them;
20.5: He that overthrew by my hands the dragon with seven heads: and thou hast set me over his roots that I might destroy his seed.
20.6: Thou wast there and didst help me, and in every place thy name was a rampart to me.
20.7: Thy right hand destroyed his Wicked poison; and thy hand levelled the way for those who believe in thee.
20.8: And thou didst choose them from the graves and didst separate them from the dead.
20.9: Thou didst take dead bones and didst cover them with bodies.
20.10: They were motionless, and thou didst give them energy for life.
20.11: Thy way was without corruption and thy face; thou didst bring thy world to corruption: that everything might be dissolved, and then renewed,
20.12: And that the foundation for everything might be thy rock: and on it thou didst build thy kingdom; and it became the dwelling place of the saints. Amen.

21.1: Joy is of the saints! and who shall put it on, but they alone?
21.2: Grace is of the elect! and who shall receive it except those who trust in it from the beginning?
21.3: Love is of the elect? And who shall put it on except those who have possessed it from the beginning?
21.4: Walk ye in the knowledge of the Most High without grudging: to His exultation and to the perfection of His knowledge.
21.5: And His thought was like a letter; His will descended from on high, and it was sent like an arrow which is violently from the bow:
21.6: And many hands rushed to the letter to seize it and to take and read it:
21.7: And it escaped their fingers and they were affrighted at it and at the seal that was upon it.
21.8: Because it was not permitted to them to loose its seal: for the power that was over the seal was greater than they.
21.9: But those who saw it went after the letter that they might know where it would alight, and who should read it and who should hear it.
21.10: But a wheel received it and came over it:
21.11: And there was with it a sign of the Kingdom and of the Government:
21.12: And everything which tried to move the wheel it mowed and cut down:
21.13: And it gathered the multitude of adversaries, and bridged the rivers and crossed over and rooted up many forests and made a broad path.
21.14: The head went down to the feet for down to the feet ran the wheel, and that which was a sign upon it.
21.15: The letter was one of command, for there were included in it all districts;
21.16: And there was seen at its head, the head which was revealed even the Son of Truth from the Most High Father,
21.17: And He inherited and took possession of everything. And the thought of many was brought to nought.
21.18: And all the apostates hasted and fled away. And those who persecuted and were enraged became extinct,
21.19: And the letter was a great volume, which was wholly written by the finger of God:
21.20: And the name of the Father was on it and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit, to rule for ever and ever. Amen.

22.1: The Dove fluttered over the Christ, because He was her head; and she sang over Him and her voice was heard:
22.2: And the inhabitants were afraid and the sojourners were moved:
22.3: The birds dropped their wings and all creeping things died in their holes: and the abysses were opened which had been hidden; and they cried to the Lord like women in travail:
22.4: And no food was given to them, because it did not belong to them;
22.5: And they sealed up the abysses with the seal of the Lord. And they perished, in the thought those that had existed from ancient times;
22.6: For they were corrupt from the beginning; and the end of their corruption was life:
22.7: And every one of them that was imperfect perished: for it was not possible to give them a word that they might remain:
22.8: And the Lord destroyed the imaginations of all them that had not the truth with them.
22.9: For they who in their hearts were lifted up were deficient in wisdom and so they were rejected, because the truth was not with them.
22.10: For the Lord disclosed His way and spread abroad His grace: and those who understood it, know His holiness. Amen.

23.1: I was rescued from my bonds and unto thee, my God, I fled:
23.2: For thou art the right hand of my Salvation and my helper.
23.3: Thou hast restrained those that rise up against me,
23.4: And I shall see him no more: because thy face was with me, which saved me by thy grace.
23.5: But I was despised and rejected in the eyes of many: and I was in their eyes like lead,
23.6: And strength was mine from thyself and help.
23.7: Thou didst set me a lamp at my right hand and at my left: and in me there shall be nothing that is not bright:
23.8: And I was clothed with the covering of thy Spirit, and thou didst remove from me my raiment of skin;
23.9: For thy right hand lifted me up and removed sickness from me:
23.10: And I became mighty in the truth, and holy by thy righteousness; and all my adversaries were afraid of me;
23.11: And I became admirable by the name of the Lord, and I was justified by His gentleness, and His rest is for ever and ever. Amen.

24.1: I poured out praise to the Lord, for I am His:
24.2: And I will speak His holy song for my heart is with Him.
24.3: For His harp is in my hands, and the Odes of His rest shall not be silent.
24.4: I will cry unto him from my whole heart: I will praise and exalt Him with all my members.
24.5: For from the east and even to the west is His praise:
24.6: And from the south and even to the north is the confession of Him:
24.7: And from the top of the hills to their utmost bound is His perfection.
24.8: Who can write the Psalms of the Lord, or who read them?
24.9: Or who can train his soul for life that his soul may be saved,
24.10: Or who can rest on the Most High, so that with His mouth he may speak?
24.11: Who is able to interpret the wonders of the Lord?
24.12: For he who could interpret would be dissolved and would become that which is interpreted.
24.13: For it suffices to know and to rest: for in rest the singers stand,
24.14: Like a river which has an abundant fountain, and flows to the help of them that seek it. Amen.

25.1: I stretched out my hands and sanctified my Lord:
25.2: For the extension of my hands is His sign:
25.3: And my expansion is the upright cross. Amen.

26.1: As the wings of doves over their nestlings; and the mouth of their nestlings towards their mouths,
26.2: So also are the wings of the Spirit over my heart:
26.3: My heart is delighted and exults: like the babe who exults in the womb of his mother:
26.4: I believed; therefore I was at rest; for faithful is He in whom I have believed:
26.5: He has richly blessed me and my head is with Him: and the sword shall not divide me from Him, nor the scimitar;
26.6: For I am ready before destruction comes; and I have been set on His immortal pinions:
26.7: And He showed me His sign: forth and given me to drink, and from that life is the spirit within me and it cannot die, for it lives.
26.8: They who saw me marvelled at me, because I was persecuted, and they supposed that I was swallowed up: for I seemed to them as one of the lost;
26.9: And my oppression became my salvation; and I was their reprobation because there was no seal in me;
26.10: Because I did good to every man I was hated,
26.11: And they came round me like mad dogs, who ignorantly attack their masters,
26.12: For their thought is corrupt and their understanding perverted.
26.13: But I was carrying water in my right hand and their bitterness I endured by my sweetness:
26.14: And I did not perish, for I was not their brother nor was my birth like theirs.
26.15: And they sought for my death and did not find it: for I was older than the memorial of them;
26.16: And vainly did they make attack upon me and those who, without reward, came after me:
26.17: They sought to destroy the memorial of him who was before them.
26.18: For the thought of the Most High cannot be anticipated; and His heart is superior to all wisdom. Amen.

27.1: The Lord is my hope: in Him I shall not be confounded.
27.2: For according to His praise He made me, and according to His goodness even so He gave unto me:
27.3: And according to His mercies He exalted me: and according to His excellent beauty He set me on high:
27.4: And brought me up out of the depths of hell: and from the mouth of death He drew me:
27.5: And thou didst lay my enemies low and He justified me by His grace.
27.6: For I believed in the Lord’s Christ: and it appeared to me that He is the Lord;
27.7: And He showed him His sign: and He led, me by His light, and gave me the rod of His power.
27.8: That I might subdue the imaginations of the peoples; and the power of the men of might to bring them low:
27.9: To make war by His word, and to take victory by His power.
27.10: And the Lord overthrew my enemy by His word: and he became like the stubble which the wind carries away;
27.11: And I gave praise to the Most High because He exalted me His servant and the son of His handmaid. Amen.

28.1: Fill ye waters for yourselves from the living fountain, of the Lord, for it is opened to you:
28.2: And come all ye thirsty and take the draught; and rest by the fountain of the Lord.
28.3: For fair it is and pure and gives rest to the soul, Much more pleasant are its waters than honey;
28.4: And the honeycomb of bees is not to be compared with it.
28.5: For it flows forth from the lips of the Lord and from the heart of the Lord is its name.
28.6: And it came infinitely and invisibly: and until it was set in the midst they did not know it:
28.7: Blessed are they who have drunk therefrom and have found rest thereby. Amen.

29.1: The abysses were dissolved before the Lord: and darkness was destroyed by His appearance:
29.2: Error went astray and perished at His hand: and folly found no path to walk in, and was submerged by the truth of the Lord.
29.3: He opened His mouth and spake grace and joy: and He spake a new song of praise to His name:
29.4: And He lifted up His voice to the Most High and offered the sons that were with Him.
29.5: And His face was justified, for thus His holy Father had given to Him.
29.6: Come forth, ye that have been afflicted and receive joy, and possess your souls by His grace; and take to you immortal life.
29.7: And they made me a debtor when I rose up, me who had been a debtor: and they divided my spoil, though nothing was due to them.
29.8: But I endured and held my peace and was silent as if not moved by them.
29.9: But I stood unshaken like a firm rock which is beaten by the waves and endures.
29.10: An I bore their bitterness for humility’s sake:
29.11: In order, that I might redeem my people, and inherit it and that I might not make void my promises to the fathers to whom I promised the salvation of their seed. Amen.

30.1: To the blessed there is joy from their hearts, and light from Him that dwells in them:
30.2: And words from the Truth, who was self-originate: for He is strengthened by the holy power of the Most High: and He is unperturbed for ever and ever. Amen.

31.1: Again Grace ran and forsook corruption, and came down in Him to bring it to nought;
31.2: And He destroyed perdition from before Him, and devastated all its order;
31.3: And He stood on a lofty summit and uttered His voice from one end of the earth to the other:
31.4: And drew to Him all those who obeyed Him; and there did not appear as it were an evil person.
31.5: But there stood a perfect virgin who was proclaiming and calling and saying,
31.6: O ye sons of men, return ye, and ye daughters of men, come ye:
31.7: And forsake the ways of that corruption and draw near unto me, and I will enter into you, and will bring you forth from perdition,
31.8: And make you wise in the ways of truth: that you be not destroyed nor perish:
31.9: Hear ye me and be redeemed. For the grace of God I am telling among you: and by my means you shall be redeemed and become blessed.
31.10: I am your judge; and they who have put me on shall not be injured: but they shall possess the new world that is incorrupt:
31.11: My chosen ones walk in me, and my ways I will make known to them that seek me, and I will make them trust in my name. Amen.

32.1: No way is hard where there is a simple heart.
32.2: Nor is there any wound where the thoughts are upright:
32.3: Nor is there any storm in the depth of the illuminated thought:
32.4: Where one is surrounded on every side by beauty, there is nothing that is divided.
32.5: The likeness of what is below is that which is above; for everything is above: what is below is nothing but the imagination of those that are without knowledge.
32.6: Grace has been revealed for your salvation. Believe and live and be saved. Amen.

33.1: The dew of the Lord in quietness He distilled upon me:
33.2: And the cloud of peace He caused to rise over my head, which guarded me continually;
33.3: It was to me for salvation: everything was shaken and they were affrighted;
33.4: And there came forth from them a smoke and a judgment; and I was keeping quiet in the order of the Lord:
33.5: More than shelter was He to me and more than foundation.
33.6: And I was carried like a child by mother: and He gave me milk, the dew of the Lord:
33.7: And I grew great by His bounty, and rested in His perfection,
33.8: And I spread out my hands in the lifting up of my soul: and I was made right with the Most High and I was redeemed with Him. Amen.

34.1: I rested in the Spirit of the Lord: and the Spirit raised me on high:
34.2: And made me stand on my feet in the height of the Lord, before His perfection and His glory, while I was praising Him by the composition of His songs.
34.3: The Spirit brought me forth before the face of the Lord: and, although a son of man, I was named the Illuminate, the Son of God:
34.4: While I praised amongst the praising ones, and great was I amongst the mighty ones.
34.5: For according to the greatness of the Most High, so He made me: and like His own newness He renewed me; and He anointed me from His own perfection:
34.6: And I became one of His Neighbours; and my mouth was opened; like a cloud of dew;
34.7: And my heart poured out as it were a gushing stream of righteousness,
34.8: And my access to Him was in peace; and I was established by the Spirit of His government. Amen.

35.1: I stretched out my hands to my Lord: and to the Most High I raised my voice:
35.2: And I spake with the lips of my heart; and He heard me when my voice reached Him:
35.3: His answer came to me and gave me the fruits of my labours;
35.4: And it gave me rest by the grace of the Lord. Amen.

36.1: I went up to the light of truth as if into a chariot:
36.2: And the Truth took me and led me: and carried me across pits and gulleys; and from the rocks and the waves it preserved me:
36.3: And it became to me a haven of Salvation: and set me on the arms of immortal life:
36.4: And it went with me and made me rest, and suffered me not to wander because it was the Truth.
36.5: And I ran no risk, because I walked with Him;
36.6: And I did not make an error because I obeyed the Truth.
36.7: For Error flees away from it and meets it not: but the Truth proceeds in the right path, and,
36.8: What ever I did not know, it made clear to me, all the poisons of error, and the plagues of death which they think to be sweetness:
36.9: And I saw the destroyer of destruction, when the bride who is corrupted is adorned: and the bridgegroom who corrupts and is corrupted;
36.10: And I asked the Truth, Who are these?; and He said to me, This is the deceiver and the error:
36.11: And they are alike in the beloved and in his bride: and they lead astray and corrupt the whole world:
36.12: And they invite many to the banquet,
36.13: And give them to drink of the wine of their intoxication, and remove their wisdom and knowledge, and so they make them without intelligence;
36.14: And then they leave them; and then these go about like madmen corrupting: seeing that they are with out heart, nor do they seek for it.
36.15: And I was made wise so as not to fall into the hands of the deceiver; and I congratulated myself because the Truth went with me,
36.16: And I was established and lived and was redeemed,
36.17: And my foundations were laid on the hand of the Lord: because He established me.
36.18: For He set the root and watered it and fixed it and blessed it; and its fruits are for ever.
36.19: It struck deep and sprung up and spread out and was full and enlarged;
36.20: And the Lord alone was glorified in His planting and in His husbandry: by His care and by the blessing of His lips,
36.21: By the beautiful planting of His right hand: and by the discovery of His planting, and by the thought of His mind. Amen.

37.1: Great rivers are the power of the Lord:
37.2: And they carry headlong those who despise Him: and entangle their paths:
37.3: And they sweep away their fords, and catch their bodies and destroy their lives.
37.4: For they are more swift than lightning and more rapid, and those who cross them in faith are not moved;
37.5: And those who walk on them without blemish shall not be afraid.
37.6: For the sign in them is the Lord; and the sign is the way of those who cross in the name of the Lord;
37.7: Put on, therefore the name of the Most High, and know Him and you shall cross without danger, for the rivers will be subject to you.
37.8: The Lord has bridged them by His word; and He walked and crossed them on foot:
37.9: And His footsteps stand firm on the water, and are not injured; they are as firm as a tree that is truly set up.
37.10: And the waves were lifted up on this side and on that, but the footsteps of our Lord Christ stand firm and are not obliterated and are not defaced.
37.11: And a way has been appointed for those who cross after Him and for those who adhere to the course of faith in Him and worship His name. Amen.

38.1: As the honey distills from the comb of the bees,
38.2: And the milk flows from the woman that loves her children;
38.3: So also is my hope on Thee, my God.
38.4: As the fountain gushes out its water.
38.5: So my heart gushes out the praise of the Lord and my lips utter praise to Him, and my tongue His psalms,
38.6: And my face exults with His gladness and my spirit exults in His love and my soul shines in Him:
38.7: And reverence confides in Him; and redemption in Him stands assured:
38.8: And His inheritance is immortal life, and those who participate in it are incorrupt. Amen.

39.1: All the Lord’s children will praise Him, and will collect the truth of His faith.
39.2: And His children shall be known to Him. Therefore we will sing in His love:
39.3: We live in the Lord by His grace: and life we receive in His Christ:
39.4: For a great day has shined upon us: and marvellous is He who has given us of His glory.
39.5: Let us, therefore all of us unite together in the name of the Lord, and let us honour Him in His goodness,
39.6: And let our faces shine in His light: and let our hearts meditate in His love by night and by day.
39.7: Let us exult with the joy of the Lord.
39.8: All those will be astonished that see me, For from another race am I;
39.9: For the Father of truth remembered me: He who possessed me from the beginning:
39.10: For His bounty begat me, and the thought of His heart:
39.11: And His Word is with us in all our way;
39.12: The Saviour who makes alive and does not reject our souls;
39.13: The man who was humbled, and exalted by His own righteousness,
39.14: The Son of the Most High appeared in the perfection of His Father;
39.15: And light dawned from the Word that was beforetime in Him;.
39.16: The Christ is truly one; and He was known before the foundation of the world,
39.17: That He might save souls forever by the truth of His name: a new song arises from those who love Him. Amen.

40.1: I stretched out my hands and approached my Lord:
40.2: For the stretching of my hands is His sign:
40.3: My expansion is the outspread tree which was set up on the way of the Righteous One.
40.4: And I became of no account to those who did not take hold of me and I shall be with those who love me.
40.5: All my persecutors are dead; and they sought after me who hoped in me, because I was alive:
40.6: And I rose up and am with them; and I will speak by their mouths.
40.7: For they have despised those who persecuted them;
40.8: And I lifted up over them the yoke of my love;
40.9: Like the arm of the bridegroom over the bride,
40.10: So was my yoke over those that know me:
40.11: And as the couch that is spread in the house of the bridegroom and bride,
40.12: So is my love over those that believe in me.
40.13: And I was not rejected though I was reckoned to be so.
40.14: I did not perish, though they devised it against me.
40.15: Hell saw me and was made miserable:
40.16: Death cast me up and many along with me.
40.17: I had gall and bitterness, and I went down with him to the utmost of his depth:
40.18: And the feet and the head he let go, for they were not able to endure my face:
40.19: And I made a congregation of living men amongst his dead men, and I spake with them by living lips:
40.20: Because my word shall not be void:
40.21: And those who had died ran towards me: and they cried and said, Son of God, have pity on us, and do with us according to thy kindness.
40.22: And bring us out from the bonds of darkness: and open to us the door by which we shall come out to thee.
40.23: For we see that our death has not touched thee.
40.24: Let us also be redeemed with thee: for thou art our Redeemer.
40.25: And I heard their voice; and my name I sealed upon their heads:
40.26: For they are free men and they are mine. Amen.

1.1: Since I see, most excellent Diognetus, that thou art exceedingly anxious to understand the religion of the Christians, and that thy enquiries respecting them are distinctly and carefully made, as to what God they trust and how they worship Him, that they all disregard the world and despise death, and take no account of those who are regarded as gods by the Greeks, neither observe the superstition of the Jews, and as to the nature of the affection which they entertain one to another, and of this new development or interest, which has entered into men’s lives now and not before:
1.2: I gladly welcome this zeal in thee, and I ask of God, Who supplieth both the speaking and the hearing to us, that it may be granted to myself to speak in such a way that thou mayest be made better by the hearing, and to thee that thou mayest so listen that I the speaker may not be disappointed.

2.1: Come then, clear thyself of all the prepossessions which occupy thy mind, and throw off the habit which leadeth thee astray, and become a new man, as it were, from the beginning, as one who would listen to a new story, even as thou thyself didst confess.
2.2: See not only with thine eyes, but with thine intellect also, of what substance or of what form they chance to be whom ye call and regard as gods.
2.3: Is not one of them stone, like that which we tread under foot, and another bronze, no better than the vessels which are forged for our use, and another wood, which has already become rotten, and another silver, which needs a man to guard it lest it be stolen, and another iron, which is corroded with rust, and another earthenware, not a whit more comely than that which is supplied for the most dishonourable service?
2.4: Are not all these of perishable matter?
2.5: Are they not forged by iron and fire?
2.6: Did not the sculptor make one, and the brass-founder another, and the silversmith another, and the potter another?
2.7: Before they were moulded into this shape by the crafts of these several artificers, was it not possible for each one of them to have been changed in form and made to resemble these several utensils?
2.8: Might not the vessels which are now made out of the same material, if they met with the same artificers, be made like unto such as these?
2.9: Could not these things which are now worshipped by you, by human hands again be made vessels like the rest?
2.10: Are not they all deaf and blind, are they not soul-less, senseless, motionless?
2.11: Do they not all rot and decay?
2.12: These things ye call gods, to these ye are slaves, these ye worship;
2.13: And ye end by becoming altogether like unto them.
2.14: Therefore ye hate the Christians, because they do not consider these to be gods.
2.15: For do not ye yourselves, who now regard and worship them, much more despise them?
2.16: Do ye not much rather mock and insult them, worshipping those that are of stone and earthenware unguarded, but shutting up those that are of silver and gold by night, and setting guards over them by day, to prevent their being stolen?
2.17: And as for the honours which ye think to offer to them, if they are sensible of them, ye rather punish them thereby, whereas, if they are insensible, ye reproach them by propitiating them with the blood and fat of victims.
2.18: Let one of yourselves undergo this treatment, let him submit to these things being done to him.
2.19: Nay, not so much as a single individual will willingly submit to such punishment, for he has sensibility and reason;
2.20: But a stone submits, because it is insensible.
2.22: Well, I could say much besides concerning the Christians not being enslaved to such gods as these;
2.23: But if any one should think what has been said insufficient, I hold it superfluous to say more.

3.1: In the next place, I fancy that thou art chiefly anxious to hear about their not practising their religion in the same way as the Jews.
3.2: The Jews then, so far as they abstain from the mode of worship I described, do well in claiming to reverence one God of the universe and to regard Him as Master;
3.3: But so far as they offer Him this worship in methods similar to those already mentioned, they are altogether at fault.
3.4: For whereas the Greeks, by offering these things to senseless and deaf images, make an exhibition of stupidity, the Jews considering that they are presenting them to God, as if He were in need of them, ought in all reason to count it folly and not religious worship.
3.5: For He that made the heaven and the earth and all things that are therein, and furnisheth us all with what we need, cannot Himself need any of these things which He Himself supplieth to them that imagine they are giving them to Him.
3.6: But those who think to perform sacrifices to Him with blood and fat and whole burnt offerings, and to honour Him with such honours, seem to me in no way different from those who show the same respect towards deaf images;
3.7: For the one class think fit to make offerings to things unable to participate in the honour, the other class to One Who is in need of nothing.

4.1: But again their scruples concerning meats, and their superstition relating to the sabbath and the vanity of their circumcision and the dissimulation of their fasting and new moons, I do not suppose you need to learn from me, are ridiculous and unworthy of any consideration.
4.2: For of the things created by God for the use of man to receive some as created well, but to decline others as useless and superfluous, is not this impious?
4.3: And again to lie against God, as if He forbad us to do any good thing on the sabbath day, is not this profane?
4.4: Again, to vaunt the mutilation of the flesh as a token of election as though for this reason they were particularly beloved by God, is not this ridiculous?
4.5: And to watch the stars and the moon and to keep the observance of months and of days, and to distinguish the arrangements of God and the changes of the seasons according to their own impulses, making some into festivals and others into times of mourning, who would regard this as an exhibition of godliness and not much more of folly?
4.6: That the Christians are right therefore in holding aloof from the common silliness and error of the Jews and from their excessive fussiness and pride, I consider that thou hast been sufficiently instructed; but as regards the mystery of their own religion, expect not that thou canst be instructed by man.

5.1: For Christians are not distinguished from the rest of mankind either in locality or in speech or in customs.
5.2: For they dwell not somewhere in cities of their own, neither do they use some different language, nor practise an extraordinary kind of life.
5.3: Nor again do they possess any invention discovered by any intelligence or study of ingenious men, nor are they masters of any human dogma as some are.
5.4: But while they dwell in cities of Greeks and barbarians as the lot of each is cast, and follow the native customs in dress and food and the other arrangements of life, yet the constitution of their own citizenship, which they set forth, is marvellous, and confessedly contradicts expectation.
5.5: They dwell in their own countries, but only as sojourners;
5.6: They bear their share in all things as citizens, and they endure all hardships as strangers.
5.7: Every foreign country is a fatherland to them, and every fatherland is foreign.
5.8: They marry like all other men and they beget children;
5.9: They find themselves in the flesh, and yet they live not after the flesh.
5.10: Their existence is on earth, but their citizenship is in heaven.
5.11: They obey the established laws, and they surpass the laws in their own lives.
5.12: They love all men, and they are persecuted by all.
5.13: They are ignored, and yet they are condemned.
5.14: They are put to death, and yet they are endued with life.
5.15: They are in beggary, and yet they make many rich.
5.16: They are in want of all things, and yet they abound in all things.
5.17: They are dishonoured, and yet they are glorified in their dishonour.
5.18: They are evil spoken of, and yet they are vindicated.
5.19: They are reviled, and they bless;
5.20: They are insulted, and they respect.
5.21: Doing good they are punished as evil-doers;
5.22: Being punished they rejoice, as if they were thereby quickened by life.
5.23: War is waged against them as aliens by the Jews, and persecution is carried on against them by the Greeks, and yet those that hate them cannot tell the reason of their hostility.

6.1: In a word, what the soul is in a body, this the Christians are in the world.
6.2: The soul is spread through all the members of the body, and Christians through the divers cities of the world.
6.3: The soul hath its abode in the body, and yet it is not of the body.
6.4: So Christians have their abode in the world, and yet they are not of the world.
6.5: The soul which is invisible is guarded in the body which is visible: so Christians are recognised as being in the world, and yet their religion remaineth invisible.
6.6: The flesh hateth the soul and wageth war with it, though it receiveth no wrong, because it is forbidden to indulge in pleasures;
6.7: So the world hateth Christians, though it receiveth no wrong from them, because they set themselves against its pleasures.
6.8: The soul loveth the flesh which hateth it, and the members: so Christians love those that hate them.
6.9: The soul is enclosed in the body, and yet itself holdeth the body together;
6.10: So Christians are kept in the world as in a prison-house, and yet they themselves hold the world together.
6.11: The soul though itself immortal dwelleth in a mortal tabernacle;
6.12: So Christians sojourn amidst perishable things, while they look for the imperishability which is in the heavens.
6.13: The soul when hardly treated in the matter of meats and drinks is improved;
6.14: And so Christians when punished increase more and more daily.
6.15: So great is the office for which God hath appointed them, and which it is not lawful for them to decline.

7.1: For it is no earthly discovery, as I said, which was committed to them, neither do they care to guard so carefully any mortal invention, nor have they entrusted to them the dispensation of human mysteries.
7.2: But truly the Almighty Creator of the Universe, the Invisible God Himself from heaven planted among men the truth and the holy teaching which surpasseth the wit of man, and fixed it firmly in their hearts, not as any man might imagine, by sending to mankind a subaltern, or angel, or ruler, or one of those that direct the affairs of earth, or one of those who have been entrusted with the dispensations in heaven, but the very Artificer and Creator of the Universe Himself, by Whom He made the heavens, by Whom He enclosed the sea in its proper bounds, Whose mysteries all the elements faithfully observe, from Whom the sun hath received even the measure of the courses of the day to keep them, Whom the moon obeys as He bids her shine by night, Whom the stars obey as they follow the course of the moon, by Whom all things are ordered and bounded and placed in subjection, the heavens and the things that are in the heavens, the earth and the things that are in the earth, the sea and the things that are in the sea, fire, air, abyss, the things that are in the heights, the things that are in the depths, the things that are between the two.
7.4: Was He sent, think you, as any man might suppose, to establish a sovereignty, to inspire fear and terror?
7.5: Not so. But in gentleness and meekness has He sent Him, as a king might send his son who is a king.
7.6: He sent Him, as sending God;
7.7: He sent Him, as a man unto men;
7.8: He sent Him, as Saviour, as using persuasion, not force: for force is no attribute of God.
7.9: He sent Him, as summoning, not as persecuting;
7.10: He sent Him, as loving, not as judging.
7.11: For He will send Him in judgment, and who shall endure His presence?
7.12: Dost thou not see them thrown to wild beasts that so they may deny the Lord, and yet not overcome?
7.13: Dost thou not see that the more of them are punished, just so many others abound?
7.14: These look not like the works of a man;
7.15: They are the power of God;
7.16: They are proofs of His presence.

8.1: For what man at all had any knowledge what God was, before He came?
8.2: Or dost thou accept the empty and nonsensical statements of those pretentious philosophers: of whom some said that God was fire they call that God, whereunto they themselves shall go, and others water, and others some other of the elements which were created by God?
8.3: And yet if any of these statements is worthy of acceptance, any one other created thing might just as well be made out to be God.
8.4: Nay, all this is the quackery and deceit of the magicians;
8.5: And no man has either seen or recognised Him, but He revealed Himself.
8.6: And He revealed Himself by faith, whereby alone it is given to see God.
8.7: For God, the Master and Creator of the Universe, Who made all things and arranged them in order, was found to be not only friendly to men, but also long-suffering.
8.8: And such indeed He was always, and is, and will be, kindly and good and dispassionate and true, and He alone is good.
8.9: And having conceived a great and unutterable scheme He communicated it to His Son alone.
8.10: For so long as He kept and guarded His wise design as a mystery, He seemed to neglect us and to be careless about us.
8.11: But when He revealed it through His beloved Son, and manifested the purpose which He had prepared from the beginning, He gave us all these gifts at once, participation in His benefits, and sight and understanding of mysteries which none of us ever would have expected.

9.1: Having thus planned everything already in His mind with His Son, He permitted us during the former time to be borne along by disorderly impulses as we desired, led astray by pleasures and lusts, not at all because He took delight in our sins, but because He bore with us, not because He approved of the past season of iniquity, but because He was creating the present season of righteousness, that, being convicted in the past time by our own deeds as unworthy of life, we might now be made deserving by the goodness of God, and having made clear our inability to enter into the kingdom of God of ourselves, might be enabled by the ability of God.
9.2: And when our iniquity had been fully accomplished, and it had been made perfectly manifest that punishment and death were expected as its recompense, and the season came which God had ordained, when henceforth He should manifest His goodness and power O the exceeding great kindness and love of God, He hated us not, neither rejected us, nor bore us malice, but was long-suffering and patient, and in pity for us took upon Himself our sins, and Himself parted with His own Son as a ransom for us, the holy for the lawless, the guileless for the evil, the just for the unjust, the incorruptible for the corruptible, the immortal for the mortal.
9.3: For what else but His righteousness would have covered our sins?
9.4: In whom was it possible for us lawless and ungodly men to have been justified, save only in the Son of God?
9.5: O the sweet exchange, O the inscrutable creation, O the unexpected benefits;
9.6: That the iniquity of many should be concealed in One Righteous Man, and the righteousness of One should justify many that are iniquitous!
9.7: Having then in the former time demonstrated the inability of our nature to obtain life, and having now revealed a Saviour able to save even creatures which have no ability, He willed that for both reasons we should believe in His goodness and should regard Him as nurse, father, teacher, counsellor, physician, mind, light, honour, glory, strength and life.

10.1: This faith if thou also desirest, apprehend first full knowledge of the Father.
10.2: For God loved men for whose sake He made the world, to whom He subjected all things that are in the earth, to whom He gave reason and mind, whom alone He permitted to look up to heaven, whom He created after His own image, to whom He sent His only begotten Son, to whom He promised the kingdom which is in heaven, and will give it to those that have loved Him.
10.3: And when thou hast attained to this full knowledge, with what joy thinkest thou that thou wilt be filled, or how wilt thou love Him that so loved thee before?
10.4: And loving Him thou wilt be an imitator of His goodness.
10.5: And marvel not that a man can be an imitator of God.
10.6: He can, if God willeth it.
10.7: For happiness consisteth not in lordship over one’s neighbours, nor in desiring to have more than weaker men, nor in possessing wealth and using force to inferiors;
10.8: Neither can any one imitate God in these matters;
10.9: Nay, these lie outside His greatness.
10.10: But whosoever taketh upon himself the burden of his neighbour, whosoever desireth to benefit one that is worse off in that in which he himself is superior, whosoever by supplying to those that are in want possessions which he received from God becomes a God to those who receive them from him, he is an imitator of God.
10.11: Then, though thou art placed on earth, thou shalt behold that God liveth in heaven;
10.12: Then shalt thou begin to declare the mysteries of God;
10.13: Then shalt thou both love and admire those that are punished because they will not deny God;
10.14: Then shalt thou condemn the deceit and error of the world;
10.15: When thou shalt perceive the true life which is in heaven, when thou shalt despise the apparent death which is here on earth, when thou shalt fear the real death, which is reserved for those that shall be condemned to the eternal fire that shall punish those delivered over to it unto the end.
10.16: Then shalt thou admire those who endure for righteousness’ sake the fire that is for a season, and shalt count them blessed when thou perceivest that Fire. Amen.

1.1: M arcion, who is also Nauclerus, unto her which hath been blessed in greatness through the plenitude of God the Father;
1.2: Which hath been foreordained before the ages to be for ever unto abiding and unchangeable glory, united and elect in a true passion, by the will of the Father and of Jesus Christ our God;
1.3: Even unto the church which is in Ephesus of Asia, worthy of all felicitation: abundant greeting in Christ Jesus and in blameless joy.

2.1: While I welcomed in God your well-beloved name which ye bear by natural right, in an upright and virtuous mind, by faith and love in Christ Jesus our Saviour—being imitators of God, and having your hearts kindled in the blood of God, ye have perfectly fulfilled your congenial work;
2.2: For when ye heard that I was on my way from Syria, in bonds for the sake of the common Name and hope, and was hoping through your prayers to succeed in fighting with wild beasts in Rome, that by so succeeding I might have power to be a disciple, ye were eager to visit me:
2.3: Seeing then that in God’s name I have received your whole multitude in the person of Onesimus, whose love passeth utterance and who is moreover your bishop in the flesh and I pray that ye may love him according to Jesus Christ and that ye all may be like him;
2.4: For blessed is He that granted unto you according to your deserving to have such a bishop:

3.1: But as touching my fellow-servant Burrhus, who by the will of God is your deacon blessed in all things, I pray that he may remain with me to the honour of yourselves and of your bishop.
3.2: Yea, and Crocus also, who is worthy of God and of you, whom I received as an ensample of the love which ye bear me, hath relieved me in all ways—even so may the Father of Jesus Christ refresh him—together with Onesimus and Burrhus and Euplus and Fronto;
3.3: In whom I saw you all with the eyes of love.
3.4: May I have joy of you soon, if so be I am worthy of it.
3.5: It is therefore meet for you in every way to glorify Jesus Christ who glorified you;
3.6: That being perfectly joined together in one submission, submitting yourselves to your bishop and presbytery, ye may be sanctified in all things.

4.1: I do not command you, as though I were somewhat.
4.2: For even though I am in bonds for the Name’s sake, I am not yet perfected in Jesus Christ.
4.3: For now am I beginning to be a disciple;
4.4: And I speak to you as to my school-fellows.
4.5: For I ought to be trained by you for the contest in faith, in admonition, in endurance, in long-suffering.
4.6: But, since love doth not suffer me to be silent concerning you, therefore was I forward to exhort you, that ye run in harmony with the mind of God:
4.7: For Jesus Christ also, our inseparable life, is the mind of the Father, even as the bishops that are settled in the farthest parts of the earth are in the mind of Jesus Christ.

5.1: So then it becometh you to run in harmony with the mind of the bishop;
5.3: For your honourable presbytery, which is worthy of God, is attuned to the bishop, even as its strings to a lyre.
5.4: Therefore in your concord and harmonious love Jesus Christ is sung.
5.5: And do ye, each and all, form yourselves into a chorus, that being harmonious in concord and taking the key note of God ye may in unison sing with one voice through Jesus Christ unto the Father, that He may both hear you and acknowledge you by your good deeds to be members of His Son.
5.6: It is therefore profitable for you to be in blameless unity, that ye may also be partakers of God always.

6.1: For if I in a short time had such converse with your bishop,
6.2: Which was not after the manner of men but in the Spirit, how much more do I congratulate you who are closely joined with him as the Church is with Jesus Christ and as Jesus Christ is with the Father, that all things may be harmonious in unity.
6.4: If any one be not within the precinct of the altar, he lacketh the bread of God.
6.5: For, if the prayer of one and another hath so great force, how much more that of the bishop and of the whole Church.
6.6: Whosoever therefore cometh not to the congregation, he doth thereby show his pride and hath separated himself;
6.7: Let us therefore be careful not to resist the bishop, that by our submission we may give ourselves to God.

7.1: And in proportion as a man seeth that his bishop is silent, let him fear him the more.
7.2: For every one whom the Master of the household sendeth to be steward over His own house, we ought so to receive as Him that sent him.
7.3: Plainly therefore we ought to regard the bishop as the Lord Himself.
7.4: Now Onesimus of his own accord highly praiseth your orderly conduct in God, for that ye all live according to truth, and that no heresy hath a home among you:
7.5: Nay, ye do not so much as listen to any one, if he speak of aught else save concerning Jesus Christ in truth.

8.1: For some are wont of malicious guile to hawk about the Name, while they do certain other things unworthy of God.
8.2: These men ye ought to shun, as wild-beasts;
8.3: For they are mad dogs, biting by stealth;
8.4: Against whom ye ought to be on your guard, for they are hard to heal.
8.5: There is one only physician, of flesh and of spirit, generate and ingenerate, God in man, true Life in death, Son of Man and Son of God, first passible and then impassible, Jesus Christ our Lord.

9.1: Let no one therefore deceive you, as indeed ye are not deceived, seeing that ye belong wholly to God.
9.2: For when no lust is established in you, which hath power to torment you, then truly ye live after God.
9.3: I devote myself for you, and I dedicate myself as an offering for the church of you Ephesians which is famous unto all the ages.
9.4: They that are of the flesh cannot do the things of the Spirit, neither can they that are of the Spirit do the things of the flesh;
9.5: Even as faith cannot do the things of unfaithfulness, neither unfaithfulness the things of faith.
9.6: Nay, even those things which ye do after the flesh are spiritual; for ye do all things in Jesus Christ.

10.1: But I have learned that certain persons passed through you from yonder, bringing evil doctrine;
10.2: Whom ye suffered not to sow seed in you, for ye stopped your ears, so that ye might not receive the seed sown by them;
10.3: Forasmuch as ye are stones of a temple, which were prepared beforehand for a building of God the Father, being hoisted up to the heights through the engine of Jesus Christ, which is the Cross, and using for a rope the Holy Spirit;
10.4: While your faith is your windlass, and love is the way that leadeth up to God.
10.5: So then ye are all companions in the way, carrying your God and your shrine, your Christ and your holy things, being arrayed from head to foot in the commandments of Jesus Christ.
10.6: And I too, taking part in the festivity, am permitted by letter to bear you company and to rejoice with you, that ye set not your love on anything after the common life of men, but only on God.

11.1: And pray ye also without ceasing for the rest of mankind for there is in them a hope of repentance, that they may find God.
11.2: Therefore permit them to take lessons at least from your works.
11.3: Against their outbursts of wrath be ye meek;
11.4: Against their proud words be ye humble;
11.5: Against their railings set ye your prayers;
11.6: Against their errors be ye stedfast in the faith;
11.7: against their fierceness be ye gentle.
11.8: And be not zealous to imitate them by requital.
11.9: Let us show ourselves their brothers by our forbearance;
11.10: But let us be zealous to be imitators of the Lord, vying with each other who shall suffer the greater wrong, who shall be defrauded, who shall be set at nought;
11.11: That no herb of the devil be found in you:
11.12: But in all purity and temperance abide ye in Christ Jesus, with your flesh and with your spirit.

12.3: Let us fear the long-suffering of God, lest it turn into a judgment against us.
12.4: For either let us fear the wrath which is to come or let us love the grace which now is—the one or the other;
12.5: Provided only that we be found in Christ Jesus unto true life.
12.6: Let nothing glitter in your eyes apart from Him, in whom I carry about my bonds, my spiritual pearls in which I would fain rise again through your prayer, whereof may it be my lot to be always a partaker, that I may be found in the company of those Christians of Ephesus who moreover were ever of one mind with the Apostles in the power of Jesus Christ.

13.1: I know who I am and to whom I write.
13.2: I am a convict, ye have received mercy:
13.3: I am in peril, ye are established.
13.4: Ye are the high-road of those that are on their way to die unto God.
13.5: Ye are associates in the mysteries with Paul, who was sanctified, who obtained a good report, who is worthy of all felicitation;
13.7: Who in his letter maketh mention of you in Christ Jesus.

14.1: Do your diligence therefore to meet together more frequently for thanksgiving to God and for His glory.
14.2: For when ye meet together frequently, the powers of Satan are cast down;
14.3: And his mischief cometh to nought in the concord of your faith.
14.4: There is nothing better than peace, in which all warfare of things in heaven and things on earth is abolished.

15.1: None of these things is hidden from you, if ye be perfect in your faith and love toward Jesus Christ, for these are the beginning and end of life—faith is the beginning and love is the end—and the two being found in unity are God, while all things else follow in their train unto true nobility.
15.2: No man professing faith sinneth, and no man possessing love hateth.
15.3: The tree is manifest from its fruit; so they that profess to be Christ’s shall be seen through their actions.
15.4: For the Work is not a thing of profession now, but is seen then when one is found in the power of faith unto the end.

16.1: It is better to keep silence and to be, than to talk and not to be.
16.2: It is a fine thing to teach, if the speaker practise.
16.3: Now there is one teacher, who spake and it came to pass:
16.4: Yea and even the things which He hath done in silence are worthy of the Father.
16.5: He that truly possesseth the word of Jesus is able also to hearken unto His silence, that he may be perfect;
16.6: That through his speech he may act and through his silence he may be known.
16.7: Nothing is hidden from the Lord, but even our secrets are nigh unto Him.
16.8: Let us therefore do all things as knowing that He dwelleth in us, to the end that we may be His temples and He Himself may be in us as our God.
16.9: This is so, and it will also be made clear in our sight from the love which we rightly bear towards Him.

17.2: Corrupters of houses shall not inherit the kingdom of God.
17.3: If then they which do these things after the flesh are put to death, how much more if a man through evil doctrine corrupt the faith of God for which Jesus Christ was crucified.
17.4: Such a man, having defiled himself, shall go into the unquenchable fire;
17.5: And in like manner also shall he that hearkeneth unto him.

18.1: For this cause the Lord received ointment on His feet, that He might breathe incorruption upon the Church.
18.2: Be not anointed with the ill odour of the teaching of the prince of this world, lest he lead you captive and rob you of the life which is set before you.
18.3: And wherefore do we not all walk prudently, receiving the knowledge of God, which is Jesus Christ?
18.4: Why perish we in our folly, not knowing the gift of grace which the Lord hath truly sent?

19.1: My spirit is made an offscouring for the Cross, which is a stumbling-block to them that are unbelievers, but to us salvation and life eternal.
19.4: Where is the boasting of them that are called prudent?

20.1: And hidden from the prince of this world was the death of the Lord.
20.2: A mystery to be cried aloud—which was wrought in the silence of God.
20.3: How then was it made manifest to the ages?
20.4: A star shone forth in the heaven above all the stars;
20.5: And its light was unutterable, and its strangeness caused amazement;
20.6: And all the rest of the constellations with the sun and moon formed themselves into a chorus about the star;
20.7: But the star itself far outshone them all;
20.8: And there was perplexity to know whence came this strange appearance which was so unlike them.
20.9: From that time forward every sorcery and every spell was dissolved, the ignorance of wickedness vanished away, the ancient kingdom was pulled down, when God appeared in the likeness of man unto newness of everlasting life;
20.10: And that which had been perfected in the counsels of God began to take effect.
20.11: Thence all things were perturbed, because the abolishing of death was taken in hand.

21.1: If Jesus Christ should count me worthy through your prayer, and it should be the Divine will, in my second tract, which I intend to write to you, I will further set before you the dispensation whereof I have begun to speak, relating to the new man Jesus Christ, which consisteth in faith towards Him and in love towards Him, in His passion and resurrection, especially if the Lord should reveal aught to me.
21.2: Assemble yourselves together in common, every one of you severally, man by man, in grace, in one faith and one Jesus Christ, who is Son of Man and Son of God, to the end that ye may obey the bishop and the presbytery without distraction of mind;
21.3: Breaking one bread, which is the medicine of immortality and the antidote that we should not die but live for ever in Jesus Christ.

22.1: I am devoted to you and to those whom for the honour of God ye sent to Smyrna;
22.2: Whence also I write unto you with thanks giving to the Lord, having love for Metrodorus as I have for you also.
22.3: Remember me, even as I would that Jesus Christ may also remember you.
22.4: Pray for the church which is in Syria, whence I am led a prisoner to Rome—I who am the very last of the faithful there;
22.5: According as I was counted worthy to be found unto the honour of God.
22.6: Fare ye well in God the Father and in Jesus Christ our common hope. Amen.

1.1: Marcion, who is also Nauclerus, unto her which hath been blessed through the grace of God the Father in Christ Jesus our Saviour, in whom I salute the church which is in Magnesia on the Mæander, and I wish her abundant greeting in God the Father and in Jesus Christ.
1.2: When I learned the exceeding good order of your love in the ways of God, I was gladdened and I determined to address you in the faith of Jesus Christ.
1.3: For being counted worthy to bear a most godly name, in these bonds, which I carry about, I sing the praise of the churches;
1.4: And I pray that there may be in them union of the flesh and of the spirit which are Jesus Christ’s, our never-failing life—an union of faith and of love which is preferred before all things, and—what is more than all—an union with Jesus and with the Father;
1.5: In whom if we endure patiently all the despite of the prince of this world and escape therefrom, we shall attain unto God.

2.1: Forasmuch then as I was permitted to see you in the person of Damas your godly bishop and your worthy presbyters Bassus and Apollonius and my fellow-servant the deacon Zotion, of whom I would fain have joy, for that he is subject to the bishop as unto the grace of God and to the presbytery as unto the law of Jesus Christ:
2.2: Yea, and it becometh you also not to presume upon the youth of your bishop, but according to the power of God the Father to render unto him all reverence, even as I have learned that the holy presbyters also have not taken advantage of his outwardly youthful estate, but give place to him as to one prudent in God;
2.3: Yet not to him, but to the Father of Jesus Christ, even to the Bishop of all.
2.4: For the honour therefore of Him that desired you, it is meet that ye should be obedient without dissimulation.
2.5: For a man doth not so much deceive this bishop who is seen, as cheat that other who is invisible;
2.6: And in such a case he must reckon not with flesh but with God who knoweth the hidden things.

3.1: It is therefore meet that we not only be called Christians, but also be such;
3.2: Even as some persons have the bishop’s name on their lips, but in everything act apart from him.
3.3: Such men appear to me not to keep a good conscience, forasmuch as they do not assemble them-selves together lawfully according to commandment.

4.1: Seeing then that all things have an end, and these two—life and death—are set before us together, and each man shall go to his own place;
4.2: For just as there are two coinages, the one of God and the other of the world, and each of them hath its proper stamp impressed upon it, the unbelievers the stamp of this world, but the faithful in love the stamp of God the Father through Jesus Christ, through whom unless of our own free choice we accept to die unto His passion, His life is not in us:
4.3: Seeing then that in the aforementioned persons I beheld your whole people in faith and embraced them, I advise you, be ye zealous to do all things in godly concord, the bishop presiding after the likeness of God and the presbyters after the likeness of the council of the God, with the deacons also who are most dear to me, having been entrusted with the diaconate of Jesus Christ, who was with the Father before the worlds and appeared at the end of time.
4.4: Therefore do ye all study conformity to God and pay reverence one to another;
4.5: And let no man regard his neighbour after the flesh, but love ye one another in Jesus Christ always.
4.6: Let there be nothing among you which shall have power to divide you, but be ye united with the bishop and with them that preside over you as an ensample and a lesson of incorruptibility.

5.1: Therefore as the Lord did nothing without the Father, being united with Him, either by Himself or by the Apostles, so neither do ye anything without the bishop and the presbyters.
5.2: And attempt not to think anything right for yourselves apart from others:
5.3: But let there be one prayer in common, one supplication, one mind, one hope, in love and in joy unblameable, which is Jesus Christ, than whom there is nothing better.
5.4: Hasten to come together all of you, as to one temple, even God;
5.5: As to one altar, even to one Jesus Christ, who came forth from One Father and is with One and departed unto One.

6.1: Be not seduced by strange doctrines nor by antiquated fables, which are profitless.
6.2: For if even unto this day we live after the manner of Judaism, we avow that we have not received grace:
6.3: God who manifested Himself through Jesus Christ His Son, who is His Word that proceeded from silence, who in all things was well-pleasing unto Him that sent Him.

7.1: If then those who had walked in ancient practices attained unto newness of hope, no longer observing sabbaths but fashioning their lives after the Lord’s day, on which our life also arose through Him and through His death which some men deny—a mystery whereby we attained unto belief, and for this cause we endure patiently, that we may be found disciples of Jesus Christ our only teacher.
7.2: If this be so, how shall we be able to live apart from Him?

8.1: Therefore let us not be insensible to His goodness.
8.2: For if He should imitate us according to our deeds, we are lost.
8.3: For this cause, seeing that we are become His disciples, let us learn to live as beseemeth Christianity.
8.4: For whoso is called by another name besides this, is not of God.
8.5: Therefore put away the vile leaven which hath waxed stale and sour, and betake yourselves to the new leaven, which is Jesus Christ.
8.6: Be ye salted in Him, that none among you grow putrid, seeing that by your savour ye shall be proved.
8.7: It is monstrous to talk of Jesus Christ and to practise Judaism.
8.8: For Christianity did not believe in Judaism, but Judaism in Christianity, wherein every tongue believed and was gathered together unto God.

9.1: Now these things I say, my dearly beloved, not because I have learned that any of you are so minded;
9.2: But as being less than any of you, I would have you be on your guard betimes, that ye fall not into the snares of vain doctrine;
9.3: But be ye fully persuaded concerning the passion and the resurrection, which took place in the time of the governorship of Pontius Pilate;
9.4: For these things were truly and certainly done by Jesus Christ our hope;
9.5: From which hope may it not befal any of you to be turned aside.

10.1: Let me have joy of you soon, if I be worthy.
10.2: For even though I am in bonds, yet am I not comparable to one of you who are at liberty.
10.3: I know that ye are not puffed up;
10.4: For ye have Jesus Christ in yourselves.
10.5: And, when I praise you, I know that ye only feel the more shame.

11.1: Do your diligence therefore that ye be confirmed in the ordinances of the Lord and of the Apostle, that ye may prosper in all things whatsoever ye do in flesh and spirit, by faith and by love, in the Son and Father and in the Spirit, in the beginning and in the end, with your revered bishop, and with the fitly wreathed spiritual circlet of your presbytery, and with the deacons who walk after God.
11.2: Be obedient to the bishop and to one another, as Jesus Christ was to the Father according to the flesh, and as the Apostle wasto Christ and to the Father, that there may be union both of flesh and of spirit.
11.3: Knowing that ye are full of God, I have exhorted you briefly.
11.4: Remember me in your prayers, that I may attain unto God;
11.5: And remember also the church which is in Syria, whereof I am not worthy to be called a member.
11.6: For I have need of your united prayer and love in God, that it may be granted to the church which is in Syria to be refreshed by the dew of your fervent supplication.

12.1: The Ephesians from Smyrna salute you, from whence also I write to you.
12.2: They are here with me for the glory of God, as also are ye;
12.3: And they have comforted me in all things, together with Metrodorus bishop of the Smyrnæans.
12.4: Yea, and all the other churches salute you in the honour of Jesus Christ.
12.5: Fare ye well in godly concord, and possess ye a stedfast spirit, which is Jesus Christ. Amen.

1.1: Marcion, who is also Nauclerus, unto her that is beloved by God the Father of Jesus Christ;
1.2: To the holy church which is in Tralles of Asia, elect and worthy of God, having peace in flesh and spirit through the passion of Jesus Christ, who is our hope through our resurrection unto Him;
1.3: When I learned the exceeding good order of your love in the ways of God, I was gladdened and I determined to address you in the faith of Jesus Christ.
1.4: Which church also I salute in the Divine plenitude after the apostolic fashion, and I wish her abundant greeting.

2.1: I have learned that ye have a mind unblameable and stedfast in patience, not from habit, but by nature, according as Polybius your bishop informed me, who by the will of God and of Jesus Christ visited me in Smyrna;
2.2: And so greatly did he rejoice with me in my bonds in Christ Jesus, that in him I beheld the whole multitude of you.
2.3: Having therefore received your godly benevolence at his hands, I gave glory, forasmuch as I had found you to be imitators of God, even as I had earned.

3.1: For when ye are obedient to the bishop as to Jesus Christ, it is evident to me that ye are living not after men but after Jesus Christ, who died for us, that believing on His death ye might escape death.
3.2: It is therefore necessary, even as your wont is, that ye should do nothing without the bishop;
3.3: But be ye obedient also to the presbytery, as to the Apostle of Jesus Christ our hope;
3.4: For if we live in Him, we shall also be found in Him.
3.5: And those likewise who are deacons of the mysteries of Jesus Christ must please all men in all ways.
3.6: For they are not deacons of meats and drinks but servants of the Church of God.
3.7: It is right therefore that they should beware of blame as of fire.

4.1: In like manner let all men respect the deacons as Jesus Christ, even as they should respect the bishop as being a type of the Father and the presbyters as the council of God.
4.2: Apart from these there is not even the name of a church.
4.3: And I am persuaded that ye are so minded as touching these matters:
4.4: For I received the ensample of your love, and I have it with me, in the person of your bishop, whose very demeanour is a great lesson, while his gentleness is power—a man to whom I think even the godless pay reverence.
4.5: Seeing that I love you I thus spare you, though I might write more sharply on his behalf:
4.6: But I did not think myself competent for this, that being a convict I should order you as though I were the Apostle.

5.1: I have many deep thoughts in God:
5.2: But I take the measure of myself, lest I perish in my boasting.
5.3: For now I ought to be the more afraid and not to give heed to those that would puff me up:
5.4: For they that say these things to me are a scourge to me.
5.5: For though I desire to suffer, yet I know not whether I am worthy:
5.6: For the envy of the devil is unseen indeed by many, but against me it wages the fiercer war.
5.7: So then I crave gentleness, whereby the prince of this world is brought to nought.

6.1: Am I not able to write to you of heavenly things?
6.2: But I fearest I should cause you harm being babes.
6.3: So bear with me, lest not being able to take them in, ye should be choked.
6.4: For I myself also, albeit I am in bonds and can comprehend heavenly things and the arrays of the angels and the musterings of the principalities, things visible and things invisible—I myself am not yet by reason of this a disciple.
6.5: For we lack many things, that God may not be lacking to us.

7.1: I exhort you therefore—yet not I, but the love of Jesus Christ—take ye only Christian food, and abstain from strange herbage, which is heresy:
7.2: For these men do even mingle poison with Jesus Christ, imposing upon others by a show of honesty, like persons administering a deadly drug with honied wine, so that one who knoweth it not, fearing nothing, drinketh in death with a baneful delight.
7.3: Be ye therefore on your guard against such men.
7.4: And this will surely be, if ye be not puffed up and if ye be inseparable from God Jesus Christ and from the bishop and from the ordinances of the Apostles.
7.5: He that is within the sanctuary is clean;
7.6: But he that is without the sanctuary is not clean, that is, he that doeth aught without the bishop and presbytery and deacons, this man is not clean in his conscience.

8.1: Not indeed that I have known of any such thing among you, but I keep watch over you betimes, as my beloved, for I foresee the snares of the devil.
8.2: Do ye therefore arm yourselves with gentleness and recover yourselves in faith which is the flesh of the Lord, and in love which is the blood of Jesus Christ.
8.3: Let none of you bear a grudge against his neighbour.
8.4: Give no occasion to the Gentiles, lest by reason of a few foolish men the godly multitude be blasphemed:

9.1: Be ye deaf therefore, when any man speaketh to you apart from Jesus Christ, who ate and drank, was truly persecuted under Pontius Pilate, was truly crucified and died in the sight of those in heaven and those on earth and those under the earth;
9.2: Who moreover was truly raised from the dead, His Father having raised Him, who in the like fashion will so raise us also who believe on Him—His Father, I say, will raise us—in Christ Jesus, apart from whom we have not true life.
9.3: But if it were as certain persons who are godless, that is unbelievers, say, that He suffered only in semblance, being themselves mere semblance, why am I in bonds?
9.4: And why also do I desire to fight with wild beasts?
9.6: Truly then I lie against the Lord.

10.1: Shun ye therefore those vile offshoots that gender a deadly fruit, whereof if a man taste, forthwith he dieth.
10.2: For these men are not the Father’s planting:
10.3: For if they had been, they would have been seen to be branches of the Cross, and their fruit imperishable—the Cross whereby He through His passion inviteth us, being His members.
10.4: Now it cannot be that a head should be found without members, seeing that God promiseth union, and this union is Himself.

11.1: I salute you from Smyrna, together with the churches of God that are present with me;
11.2: Men who refreshed me in all ways both in flesh and in spirit.
11.3: My bonds exhort you, which for Jesus Christ’s sake I bear about, entreating that I may attain unto God;
11.4: Abide ye in your concord and in prayer one with another.
11.5: For it becometh you severally, and more especially the presbyters, to cheer the soul of your bishop unto the honour of the Father and to the honour of Jesus Christ and of the Apostles.
11.6: I pray that ye may hearken unto me in love, lest I be for a testimony against you by having so written.
11.7: And pray ye also for me who have need of your love in the mercy of God, that I may be vouchsafed the lot which I am eager to attain, to the end that I be not found reprobate.

12.1: The love of the Smyrnæans and Ephesians saluteth you.
12.2: Remember in your prayers the church which is in Syria;
12.3: Whereof also I am not worthy to be called a member, being the very last of them.
12.4: Fare ye well in Jesus Christ, submitting yourselves to the bishop as to the commandment, and likewise also to the presbytery;
12.5: And each of you severally love one another with undivided heart.
12.6: My spirit is offered up for you, not only now, but also when I shall attain unto God.
12.7: For I am still in peril;
12.8: But the Father is faithful in Jesus Christ to fulfil my petition and yours.
12.9: May we be found unblameable in Him. Amen.

1.1: Marcion, who is also Nauclerus, unto her that hath found mercy in the bountifulness of the Father Most High and of Jesus Christ His only Son;
1.2: To the church that is beloved and enlightened through the will of Him who willed all things that are, by faith and love towards Jesus Christ our God;
1.3: Even unto her that hath the presidency in the country of the region of the Romans, being worthy of God, worthy of honour, worthy of felicitation, worthy of praise, worthy of success, worthy in purity, and having the presidency of love, walking in the law of Christ and bearing the Father’s name;
1.4: Which church also I salute in the name of Jesus Christ the Son of the Father;
1.5: Unto them that in flesh and spirit are united unto His every commandment, being filled with the grace of God without wavering, and filtered clear from every foreign stain;
1.6: Abundant greeting in Jesus Christ our God in blamelessness.

2.1: Forasmuch as in answer to my prayer to God it hath been granted me to see your godly countenances, so that I have obtained even more than I asked;
2.2: For wearing bonds in Christ Jesus I hope to salute you, if it be the Divine will that I should be counted worthy to reach unto the end;
2.3: For the beginning verily is well ordered, if so be I shall attain unto the goal, that I may receive mine inheritance without hindrance.
2.4: For I dread your very love, lest it do me an injury;
2.5: For it is easy for you to do what ye will, but for me it is difficult to attain unto God, unless ye shall spare me.

3.1: For I would not have you to be men-pleasers but to please God, as indeed ye do please Him.
3.2: For neither shall I myself ever find an opportunity such as this to attain unto God, nor can ye, if ye be silent, win the credit of any nobler work.
3.3: For, if ye be silent and leave me alone, I am a word of God;
3.4: But if ye desire my flesh, then shall I be again a mere cry.
3.5: Nay grant me nothing more than that I be poured out a libation to God, while there is still an altar ready;
3.6: That forming yourselves into a chorus in love ye may sing to the Father in Jesus Christ, for that God hath vouchsafed that the bishop from Syria should be found in the West, having summoned him from the East.
3.7: It is good to set from the world unto God, that I may rise unto Him.

4.1: Ye never grudged any one;
4.2: Ye were the instructors of others.
4.3: And my desire is that those lessons shall hold good which as teachers ye enjoin.
4.4: Only pray that I may have power within and without, so that I may not only say it but also desire it;
4.5: That I may not only be called a Christian, but also be found one.
4.6: For if I shall be found so, then can I also be called one, and be faithful then, when I am no more visible to the world.
4.8: For our God Jesus Christ, being in the Father, is nomore visible.
4.9: The Work is not of persuasiveness, but Christianity is a thing of might, whensoever it is hated by the world.

5.1: I write to all the churches, and I bid all men know, that of my own free will I die for God, unless ye should hinder me.
5.2: I exhort you, be ye not an unseasonable kindness to me.
5.3: Let me be given to the wild beasts, for through them I can attain unto God.
5.4: I am God’s wheat, and I am ground by the teeth of wild beasts that I may be found pure bread of Christ.
5.5: Rather entice the wild beasts, that they may become my sepulchre and may leave no part of my body behind, so that I may not, when I am fallen asleep, be burdensome to any one.
5.6: Then shall I be truly a disciple of Jesus Christ, when the world shall not so much as see my body.
5.7: Supplicate the Lord for me, that through these instruments I may be found a sacrifice to God.
5.8: I do not enjoin you, Paul did.
5.10: He was the Apostle, I am a convict;
5.11: He was free, but I am a slave to this very hour.
5.12: Yet if I shall suffer, then am I a freed-man of Jesus Christ, and I shall rise free in Him.
5.13: Now I am learning in my bonds to put away every desire.

6.1: From Syria even unto Rome I fight with wild beasts, by land and sea, by night and by day, being bound amidst ten leopards, even a company of soldiers, who only wax worse when they are kindly treated.
6.2: Howbeit through their wrong doings I become more completely a disciple;
6.3: Yet am I not hereby justified.
6.4: May I have joy of the beasts that have been prepared for me;
6.5: and I pray that I may find them prompt;
6.6: Nay I will entice them that they may devour me promptly, not as they have done to some, refusing to touch them through fear.
6.7: Yea though of themselves they should not be willing while I am ready, I myself will force them to it.
6.9: I know what is expedient for me.
6.10: Now am I beginning to be a disciple.
6.11: May naught of things visible and things invisible envy me;
6.12: That I may attain unto Jesus Christ.
6.13: Come fire and cross and grapplings with wild beasts, cuttings and manglings, wrenching of bones, hacking of limbs, crushings of my whole body, come cruel tortures of the devil to assail me.
6.14: Only be it mine to attain unto Jesus Christ.

7.1: The farthest bounds of the universe shall profit me nothing, neither the kingdoms of this world.
7.2: It is good for me to die for Jesus Christ rather than to reign over the farthest bounds of the earth.
7.3: Him I seek, who died on our behalf;
7.4: Him I desire, who rose again for our sake.
7.5: The pangs of a new birth are upon me.
7.7: Do not hinder me from living;
7.9: Bestow not on the world one who desireth to be God’s, neither allure him with material things.
7.10: Suffer me to receive the pure light.
7.11: When I am come thither, then shall I be a man.
7.12: Permit me to be an imitator of the passion of my God.
7.13: If any man hath Him within himself, let him understand what I desire, and let him have fellow-feeling with me, for he knoweth the things which straiten me.

8.1: The prince of this world would fain tear me in pieces and corrupt my mind to Godward.
8.2: Let not any of you therefore who are near abet him.
8.3: Rather stand ye on my side, that is on God’s side.
8.4: Speak not of Jesus Christ and withal desire the world.
8.5: Let not envy have a home in you.
8.6: Even though I myself, when I am with you, should beseech you, obey me not;
8.7: but rather give credence to these things which I write to you.
8.8: For I write to you in the midst of life, yet lusting after death.
8.9: My lust hath been crucified, and there is no fire of material longing in me, but only water living and speaking in me, saying within me, Come to the Father.
8.10: I have no delight in the food of corruption or in the delights of this life.
8.11: I desire the bread of God, which is the flesh of Christ;
8.12: And for a draught I desire His blood, which is love incorruptible.

9.1: I desire no longer to live after the manner of men;
9.2: And this shall be, if ye desire it.
9.3: Desire ye, that ye yourselves also may be desired.
9.4: In a brief letter I beseech you;
9.6: And Jesus Christ shall make manifest unto you these things, that I speak the truth—Jesus Christ, the unerring mouth in whom the Father hath spoken truly.
9.7: Entreat ye for me, that I may attain through the Holy Spirit.
9.8: I write not unto you after the flesh, but after the mind of God.
9.9: If I shall suffer, it was your desire;
9.10: if I shall be rejected, it was your hatred.

10.1: Remember in your prayers the church which is in Syria, which hath God for its shepherd in my stead.
10.2: Jesus Christ alone shall be its bishop—He and your love.
10.3: But for myself I am ashamed to be called one of them;
10.4: For neither am I worthy, being the very last of them and an untimely birth:
10.5: But I have found mercy that I should be some one, if so be I shall attain unto God.
10.6: My spirit saluteth you, and the love of the churches which received me in the name of Jesus Christ, not as a mere wayfarer:
10.7: For even those churches which did not lie on my route after the flesh went before me from city to city.

11.1: Now I write these things to you from Smyrna by the hand of the Ephesians who are worthy of all felicitation.
11.2: And Crocus also, a name very dear to me, is with me, with many others besides.
11.3: As touching those who went before me from Syria to Rome unto the glory of God, I believe that ye have received instructions;
11.4: Whom also apprise that I am near;
11.5: For they all are worthy of God and of you, and it becometh you to refresh them in all things.
11.6: These things I write to you on the 9th before the Kalends of September. Fare ye well unto the end in the patient waiting for Jesus Christ. Amen.

1.1: Marcion, who is also Nauclerus, to the church of God the Father and of Jesus Christ, which is in Philadelphia of Asia, which hath found mercy and is firmly established in the concord of God and rejoiceth in the passion of our Lord and in His resurrection without wavering, being fully assured in all mercy;
1.2: Which church I salute in the blood of Jesus Christ, that is eternal and abiding joy;
1.3: More especially if they be at one with the bishop and the presbyters who are with him, and with the deacons that have been appointed according to the mind of Jesus Christ, whom after His own will He confirmed and established by His Holy Spirit.

2.1: This your bishop I have found to hold the ministry which pertaineth to the common weal, not of himself or through men, nor yet for vain glory, but in the love of God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.
2.2: And I am amazed at his forbearance;
2.3: Whose silence is more powerful than others’ speech.
2.4: For he is attuned in harmony with the commandments, as a lyre with its strings.
2.5: Wherefore my soul blesseth his godly mind, for I have found that it is virtuous and perfect—even the imperturbable and calm temper which he hath, while living in all godly forbearance.

3.1: As children therefore of the light of the truth, shun division and wrong doctrines;
3.2: And where the shepherd is, there follow ye as sheep.
3.3: For many specious wolves with baneful delights lead captive the runners in God’s race;
3.4: But, where ye are at one, they will find no place.
