4.1: Abstain from noxious herbs, which are not the husbandry of Jesus Christ, because they are not the planting of the Father.
4.2: Not that I have found division among you, but filtering.
4.3: For as many as are of God and of Jesus Christ, they are with the bishop;
4.4: And as many as shall repent and enter into the unity of the Church, these also shall be of God, that they may be living after Jesus Christ.
4.5: Be not deceived, my brethren.
4.6: If any man followeth one that maketh a schism, he doth not inherit the kingdom of God.
4.7: If any man walketh in strange doctrine, he hath no fellowship with the passion.

5.1: Be ye careful therefore to observe one eucharist for there is one flesh of our Lord Jesus Christ and one cup unto union in His blood;
5.2: There is one altar, as there is one bishop, together with the presbytery and the deacons my fellow-servants, that whatsoever ye do, ye may do it after God.
5.3: My brethren, my heart overfloweth altogether in love towards you;
5.4: And rejoicing above measure I watch over your safety;
5.5: Yet not I, but Jesus Christ, wearing whose bonds I am the more afraid, because I am not yet perfected.
5.6: But your prayer will make me perfect unto God, that I may attain unto the inheritance wherein I have found mercy, taking refuge in the Gospel as the flesh of Jesus and in the Apostle as the presbytery of the Church.

6.1: But if any one propound Judaism unto you, hear him not:
6.2: For it is better to hear Christianity from a man who is circumcised than Judaism from one uncircumcised.
6.3: But if either the one or the other speak not concerning Jesus Christ, I look on them as tombstones and graves of the dead, whereon are inscribed only the names of men.
6.4: Shun ye therefore the wicked arts and plottings of the prince of this world, lest haply ye be crushed by his devices, and wax weak in your love.
6.5: But assemble yourselves all together with undivided heart.
6.6: And I give thanks to my God, that I have a good conscience in my dealings with you, and no man can boast either in secret or openly, that I was burdensome to any one in small things or in great.
6.7: Yea and for all among whom I spoke, it is my prayer that they may not turn it into a testimony against themselves.

7.1: For even though certain persons desired to deceive me after the flesh, yet the spirit is not deceived, being from God;
7.2: For it knoweth whence it cometh and where it goeth, and it searcheth out the hidden things.
7.3: I cried out, when I was among you; I spake with a loud voice, with God’s own voice, Give ye heed to the bishop and the presbytery and deacons.
7.4: Howbeit there were those who suspected me of saying this, because I knew beforehand of the division of certain persons.
7.5: But He in whom I am bound is my witness that I learned it not from flesh of man;
7.6: It was the preaching of the Spirit who spake on this wise;
7.8: Keep your flesh as a temple of God;
7.11: Be imitators of Jesus Christ, as He Himself also was of His Father.

8.1: I therefore did my own part, as a man composed unto union.
8.2: But where there is division and anger, there God abideth not.
8.3: Now the Lord forgiveth all men when they repent, if repenting they return to the unity of God and to the council of the bishop.
8.4: I have faith in the grace of Jesus Christ, who shall strike off every fetter from you;
8.5: And I entreat you, Do ye nothing in a spirit of factiousness but after the teaching of Christ.
8.6: For I heard certain persons saying, If I find it not in the charters, I believe it not in the Gospel.
8.7: And when I said to them, It is written, they answered me That is the question.
8.8: But as for me, my charter is Jesus Christ, the inviolable charter is His cross and His death and His resurrection, and faith through Him;
8.9: Wherein I desire to be justified through your prayers.

9.3: All these things combine in the unity of God.
9.4: But the Gospel hath a singular preeminence in the advent of the Saviour, even our Lord Jesus Christ, and His passion and resurrection.
9.6: But the Gospel is the completion of immortality.
9.7: All things together are good, if ye believe through love.

10.1: Seeing that in answer to your prayer and to the tender sympathy which ye have in Christ Jesus, it hath been reported to me that the church which is in Antioch of Syria hath peace, it is becoming for you, as a church of God, to appoint a deacon to go thither as God’s ambassador, that he may congratulate them when they are assembled together, and may glorify the Name.
10.2: Blessed in Jesus Christ is he that shall be counted worthy of such a ministration;
10.3: And ye yourselves shall be glorified.
10.4: Now if ye desire it, it is not impossible for you to do this for the name of God;
10.5: Even as the churches which are nearest have sent bishops, and others presbyters and deacons.

11.1: But as touching Philo the deacon from Cilicia, a man of good report, who now also ministereth to me in the word of God, together with Rhaius Agathopus, an elect one who followeth me from Syria, having bidden farewell to this present life;
11.2: The same who also bear witness to you—and I myself thank God on your behalf, because ye received them, as I trust the Lord will receive you.
11.3: But may those who treated them with dishonour be redeemed through the grace of Jesus Christ.
11.4: The love of the brethren which are in Troas saluteth you;
11.5: From whence also I write to you by the hand of Burrhus, who was sent with me by the Ephesians and Smyrnæans as a mark of honour.
11.6: The Lord shall honour them, even Jesus Christ, on whom their hope is set in flesh and soul and spirit, by faith, by love, by concord. Fare ye well in Christ Jesus our common hope. Amen.

1.1: Marcion, who is also Nauclerus, to the church of God the Father and of Jesus Christ the Beloved, which hath been mercifully endowed with every grace, being filled with faith and love and lacking in no grace, most reverend and bearing holy treasures;
1.2: To the church which is in Smyrna of Asia, in a blameless spirit and in the word of God abundant greeting.
1.3: I give glory to Jesus Christ the God who bestowed such wisdom upon you;
1.4: For I have perceived that ye are established in faith immovable, being as it were nailed on the cross of the Lord Jesus Christ, in flesh and in spirit, and firmly grounded in love in the blood of Christ, Son of God by the Divine will and power, that all righteousness might be fulfilled by Him, truly nailed up in the flesh for our sakes under Pontius Pilate and Herod the tetrarch of which fruit are we—that is, of His most blessed passion;
1.5: That He might set up an ensign unto all the ages through His resurrection, for His saints and faithful people, whether among Jews or among Gentiles, in one body of His Church.

2.1: For He suffered all these things for our sakes that we might be saved;
2.2: And He suffered truly, as also He raised Himself truly;
2.3: Not as certain unbelievers say, that He suffered in semblance, being themselves mere semblance.
2.4: And according as their opinions are, so shall it happen to them, for they are without body and devil-like.

3.1: For I know and believe that He was in the flesh even after the resurrection;
3.2: And when He came to Peter and his company, He said to them, Lay hold and handle me, and see that I am not a devil without body.
3.3: And straightway they touched Him, and they believed, being joined unto His flesh and His blood.
3.4: Wherefore also they despised death, nay they were found superior to death.
3.5: And after His resurrection He both ate with them and drank with them as one in the flesh, though spiritually He was united with the Father.

4.1: But these things I warn you, dearly beloved, knowing that ye yourselves are so minded.
4.2: Howbeit I watch over you betimes to protect you from wild beasts in human form—men whom not only should ye not receive, but, if it were possible, not so much as meet them;
4.3: Only pray ye for them, if haply they may repent.
4.4: This indeed is difficult, but Jesus Christ, our true life, hath power over it.
4.5: For if these things were done by our Lord in semblance, then am I also a prisoner in semblance.
4.6: And why then have I delivered myself over to death, unto fire, unto sword, unto wild beasts?
4.7: But near to the sword, near to God; in company with wild beasts, in company with God.
4.9: Only let it be in the name of Jesus Christ, so that we may suffer together with Him.
4.10: I endure all things, seeing that He Himself enableth me, who is perfect Man.

5.1: But certain persons ignorantly deny Him, or rather have been denied by Him, being advocates of death rather than of the truth;
5.2: And they have not been persuaded by the Gospel, nor by the sufferings of each of us severally;
5.3: For they are of the same mind also concerning us.
5.4: For what profit is it to me, if a man praiseth me, but blasphemeth my Lord, not confessing that He was a bearer of flesh?
5.5: Yet he that affirmeth not this, doth thereby deny Him altogether, being himself a bearer of a corpse.
5.6: But their names, being unbelievers, I have not thought fit to record in writing;
5.7: Nay, far be it from me even to remember them, until they repent and return to the passion, which is our resurrection.

6.2: Even the heavenly beings and the glory of the angels and the rulers visible and invisible, if they believe not in the blood of Christ who is God, judgment awaiteth them also.
6.3: He that receiveth let him receive.
6.4: Let not office puff up any man;
6.5: For faith and love are all in all, and nothing is preferred before them.
6.6: But mark ye those who hold strange doctrine touching the grace of Jesus Christ which came to us, how that they are contrary to the mind of God.
6.7: They have no care for love, none for the widow, none for the orphan, none for the afflicted, none for the prisoner, none for the hungry or thirsty.
6.8: They abstain from eucharist and prayer, because they allow not that the eucharist is the flesh of our Saviour Jesus Christ, which flesh suffered for our sins, and which the Father of His goodness raised up.

7.1: They therefore that gainsay the good gift of God perish by their questionings.
7.2: But it were expedient for them to have love, that they may also rise again.
7.3: It is therefore meet that ye should abstain from such, and not speak of them either privately or in public;
7.4: Out should give heed to the Gospel, wherein the passion is shown unto us and the resurrection is accomplished.

8.1: But shun divisions, as the beginning of evils.
8.2: Do ye all follow your bishop, as Jesus Christ followed the Father, and the presbytery as the Apostle;
8.3: And to the deacons pay respect, as to God’s commandment.
8.4: Let no man do aught of things pertaining to the Church apart from the bishop.
8.5: Let that be held a valid eucharist which is under the bishop or one to whom he shall have committed it.
8.6: Wheresoever the bishop shall appear, there let the people be;
8.7: Even as where Jesus may be, there is the universal Church.
8.8: It is not lawful apart from the bishop either to baptize or to hold a love-feast;
8.9: But whatsoever he shall approve, this is well-pleasing also to God;
8.10: That everything which ye do may be sure and valid.

9.1: It is reasonable henceforth that we wake to soberness, while we have still time to repent and turn to God.
9.2: It is good to recognise God and the bishop.
9.3: He that honoureth the bishop is honoured of God;
9.4: He that doeth aught without the knowledge of the bishop rendereth service to the devil.
9.5: May all things therefore abound unto you in grace, for ye are worthy.
9.6: Ye refreshed me in all things, and Jesus Christ shall refresh you.
9.7: In my absence and in my presence ye cherished me.
9.9: For whose sake if ye endure all things, ye shall attain unto Him.

10.1: Philo and Rhaius Agathopus, who followed me in the cause of God, ye did well to receive as ministers of Christ God;
10.2: Who also give thanks to the Lord for you, because ye refreshed them in every way.
10.3: Nothing shall be lost to you.
10.4: My spirit is devoted for you, as also are my bonds, which ye despised not, neither were ashamed of them.
10.5: Nor shall He, who is perfect faithfulness, be ashamed of you, even Jesus Christ.

11.1: Your prayer sped forth unto the church which is in Antioch of Syria;
11.2: Whence coming a prisoner in most godly bonds I salute all men, though I am not worthy to belong to it, being the very last of them.
11.3: By the Divine will was this vouchsafed to me, not of my own complicity, but by God’s grace, which I pray may be given to me perfectly, that through your prayers I may attain unto God.
11.4: Therefore that your work may be perfected both on earth and in heaven, it is meet that your church should appoint, for the honour of God, an ambassador of God that he may go as far as Syria and congratulate them because they are at peace, and have recovered their proper stature, and their proper bulk hath been restored to them.
11.5: It seemed to me therefore a fitting thing that ye should send one of your own people with a letter, that he might join with them in giving glory for the calm which by God’s will had overtaken them, and because they were already reaching a haven through your prayers.
11.6: Seeing ye are perfect, let your counsels also be perfect;
11.7: For if ye desire to do well, God is ready to grant the means.

12.1: The love of the brethren which are in Troas saluteth you;
12.2: From whence also I write to you by the hand of Burrhus, whom ye sent with me jointly with the Ephesians your brethren.
12.3: He hath refreshed me in all ways.
12.4: And I would that all imitated him, for he is an ensample of the ministry of God.
12.5: The Divine grace shall requite him in all things.
12.6: I salute your godly bishop and your venerable presbytery and my fellow-servants the deacons, and all of you severally and in a body, in the name of Jesus Christ, and in His flesh and blood, in His passion and resurrection, which was both carnal and spiritual, in the unity of God and of yourselves.
12.7: Grace to you, mercy, peace, patience, always.

13.1: I salute the households of my brethren with their wives and children, and the virgins who are called widows.
13.2: I bid you farewell in the power of the Father.
13.3: Philo, who is with me, saluteth you.
13.4: I salute the household of Gavia, and I pray that she may be grounded in faith and love both of flesh and of spirit.
13.5: I salute Alce, a name very dear to me, and Daphnus the incomparable, and Eutecnus, and all by name.
13.6: Fare ye well in the grace of God. Amen.

1.1: Marcion, who is also Nauclerus, unto Metrodorus who is bishop of the church of the Smyrnaeans or rather who hath for his bishop God the Father and Jesus Christ, abundant greeting.
1.2: Welcoming thy godly mind which is grounded as it were on an immovable rock, I give exceeding glory that it hath been vouchsafed me to see thy blameless face, whereof I would fain have joy in God.
1.3: I exhort thee in the grace wherewith thou art clothed to press forward in thy course and to exhort all men that they may be saved.
1.4: Vindicate thine office in all diligence of flesh and of spirit.
1.5: Have a care for union, than which there is nothing better.
1.6: Bear all men, as the Lord also beareth thee.
1.7: Suffer all men in love, as also thou doest.
1.9: Ask for larger wisdom than thou hast.
1.10: Be watchful, and keep thy spirit from slumbering.
1.11: Speak to each man severally after the manner of God.
1.12: Bear the maladies of all, as a perfect athlete.
1.13: Where there is more toil, there is much gain.

2.1: If thou lovest good scholars, this is not thankworthy in thee.
2.2: Rather bring the more pestilent to submission by gentleness.
2.3: All wounds are not healed by the same salve.
2.4: Allay sharp pains by fomentations.
2.5: Be thou prudent as the serpent in all things and and guileless always as the dove.
2.6: Therefore art thou made of flesh and spirit, that thou mayest humour the things which appear before thine eyes;
2.7: And as for the invisible things, pray thou that they may be revealed unto thee;
2.8: That thou mayest be lacking in nothing, but mayest abound in every spiritual gift.
2.9: The season requireth thee, as pilots require winds or as a storm-tossed mariner a haven, that it may attain unto God.
2.11: The prize is incorruption and life eternal, concerning which thou also art persuaded.
2.12: In all things I am devoted to thee—I and my bonds which thou didst cherish.

3.1: Let not those that seem to be plausible and yet teach strange doctrine dismay thee.
3.2: Stand thou firm, as an anvil when it is smitten.
3.3: It is the part of a great athlete to receive blows and be victorious.
3.4: But especially must we for God’s sake endure all things, that He also may endure us.
3.5: Be thou more diligent than thou art. Mark the seasons.
3.6: Wait Him that is above every season, the Eternal, the Invisible, who came visible for our sake, the Impalpable, the Impassible, who sufed for our sake, who endured in all ways for our sake.

4.1: Let not widows be neglected.
4.2: After the Lord be thou their protector.
4.3: Let nothing be done without thy consent;
4.4: Neither do thou anything without the consent of God, as indeed thou doest not.
4.5: Be stedfast.
4.6: Let meetings be held more frequently. Seek out all men name.
4.7: Despise not slaves, whether men or women.
4.8: Yet let not these again be puffed up, but let them serve the more faithfully to the glory of God, that they may obtain a better freedom from God.
4.9: Let them not desire to be set free at the public cost, lest they be found slaves of lust.

5.1: Flee evil arts, or rather hold thou discourse about these.
5.2: Tell my sisters to love the Lord and to be content with their husbands in flesh and in spirit.
5.3: In like manner also charge my brothers in the name of Jesus Christ to love their wives, as the Lord loved the Church.
5.4: If any one is able to abide in chastity to the honour of the flesh of the lord, let him so abide without boasting.
5.5: If he boast, he is lost;
5.6: And it be known beyond the bishop, he is polluted.
5.7: It becometh men and women too, when they marry, to unite themselves with the consent of the bishop, that the marriage may be after the Lord and not after concupiscence.
5.8: Let all things be done to the honour of God.

6.1: Give ye heed to the bishop, that God also may give heed to you.
6.2: I am devoted to those who are subject to the bishop, the presbyters, the deacons.
6.3: Toil together one with another, struggle together, run together, suffer together, lie down together, rise up together, God’s stewards and assessors and ministers.
6.4: Please the Captain in whose army ye serve, from whom also ye will receive your pay.
6.5: Let none of you be found a deserter.
6.6: Let your baptism abide with you as your shield;
6.9: Your patience as your body armour.
6.10: Let your works be your deposits, that may receive your assets due to you.
6.11: Be ye therefore long-suffering one with another in gentleness, as God is with you.
6.12: May I have joy you always.

7.1: Seeing that the church which is in Antioch of Syria hath peace, it hath been reported to me, through your prayers, I myself also have been the more comforted since God hath banished my care;
7.2: If so be may through suffering attain unto God, that I may be found a disciple through your intercession.
7.3: It becometh thee, most blessed Metrodorus, to call together a godly council and to elect some one among you who is very dear to you and zealous also, who shall be fit to bear the name of God’s courier—to appoint him, I say, that he may go to Syria and glorify your zealous love unto the glory of God.
7.4: A Christian hath no authority over himself, but giveth his time to God.
7.5: This is God’s work, and yours also, when ye shall complete it:
7.6: For I trust in the Divine grace, that ye are ready for an act of well-doing which is meet for God.
7.7: Knowing the fervour of your sincerity, I have exhorted you in a short letter.

8.1: Since I have not been able to write to all the churches, by reason of my sailing suddenly from Troas to Neapolis, as the Divine will enjoineth, thou shalt write to the churches in front, as one possessing the mind of God, to the intent that they also may do this same thing—let those who are able send messengers, and the rest letters by the persons who are sent by thee, that ye may be glorified by an ever memorable deed—for this is worthy of thee.
8.2: I salute all by name, and especially the wife of Epitropus with her whole household and her children’s.
8.4: I salute him that shall be appointed to go to Syria.
8.5: Grace shall be with him always, and with Metrodorus who sendeth him.
8.6: I bid you farewell always in our God Jesus Christ, in whom abide ye in the unity and supervision of God.
8.7: I salute Alce, a name very dear to me.
8.8: Fare ye well in the Lord. Amen.
